img::images/books/DSA - 3_page188_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates a linked-list-based algorithm for processing equivalence relations, where input pairs are used to construct a forest of disjoint sets. The `seq` array acts as a parent pointer array, with each index `i` pointing to the representative (root) of the equivalence class containing element `i`, while the `data` field in each node stores the element value. The algorithm iterates through the array, identifying and outputting connected components (equivalence classes) by following parent pointers until reaching roots, which are marked as `NULL` in the `link` field. The `out` array tracks visited nodes to avoid redundant output, ensuring each equivalence class is printed exactly once.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page189_img1.p.caption: This code implements a Union-Find data structure using linked lists to manage equivalence classes. Key components include `seq[]` (an array of pointers to linked-list heads), `out[]` (a boolean array tracking class membership), and `node` structures with `data` and `link` fields. The algorithm processes input pairs to union elements, then traverses each list to output connected components, leveraging pointer manipulation and stack-like traversal to maintain set integrity.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page18_img1.pn.caption: This preface page acknowledges editorial and publishing support, highlighting key personnel roles such as Acquisitions Editor and Associate Managing Editor. It emphasizes the collaborative nature of textbook production, where editorial leadership (e.g., Nola Hague and Penny Hull) plays a crucial role in project success. The document serves as a meta-level acknowledgment, illustrating the human and organizational infrastructure behind technical content creation.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page190_img1.p.caption: This figure presents a linked-list-based algorithm for finding equivalence classes, demonstrating the union-find data structure’s amortized efficiency. The key components are a stack (`top`) for managing nodes and a sequence (`seq`) for storing equivalence pairs, with the algorithm using a linked stack to process nodes in O(m + n) time. The critical relationship is that each node is pushed onto the stack once and popped once, ensuring linear time complexity for the entire process, which is optimal for equivalence relation processing.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page191_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates a compressed sparse row (CSR) matrix representation using a linked list structure. Key components include head nodes storing row/column dimensions and entry nodes containing value, row, column, and pointers (down, right) to adjacent non-zero elements. Entry nodes are doubly linked within their respective row and column lists, enabling efficient traversal of non-zero elements while minimizing memory usage for sparse matrices.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page192_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates a linked list-based sparse matrix representation, where non-zero elements are stored as nodes in a doubly-linked structure. Each `entry-node` contains row, column, and value fields, while `matrix-node` headers (`H0-H3`) manage row-wise linked lists via `down` pointers and connect adjacent rows via `right` pointers. The union in `matrix-node` enables space-efficient storage by alternating between header and entry node types based on the `tagfield`, facilitating traversal and sparse matrix operations.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page193_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates the input format for constructing a sparse matrix’s linked list representation, a data structure optimized for matrices with many zero elements. The key components are the head nodes (stored in the `hdnode` array) that anchor each column’s linked list, and the `next` field that links nodes within a column. The input data, ordered by row and then column, is processed to build these lists, establishing a direct relationship between the sparse matrix’s nonzero elements and their corresponding linked list nodes.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page194_img1.p.caption: This code implements a linked-list-based sparse matrix representation, specifically the Compressed Sparse Row (CSR) format. Key components include head nodes (`hdnode[]`) that manage row lists, and entry nodes that store non-zero values with their row and column indices. The algorithm dynamically builds the matrix by linking new entries into row and column lists, then connecting the head nodes to form a contiguous structure for efficient traversal. The `last` pointer tracks the tail of the current row, and the `right` and `down` pointers maintain row-wise and column-wise connectivity.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page195_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates the allocation of a new node in a sparse matrix representation, a core component of dynamic memory management in linked data structures. The `new-node` function uses `malloc` to allocate memory for a `matrix-node`, checking for allocation failure via `IS-FULL` before returning a pointer. This operation is foundational for building the sparse matrix’s linked structure, where nodes represent non-zero elements, and the `next` pointer facilitates traversal within rows, enabling efficient storage and access patterns.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page196_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates a sparse matrix traversal algorithm using a linked-list-based row-major representation. The key concept is efficient memory access via a doubly-linked list structure where each node stores row, column, and value, and the `head->right` pointer chains nodes within a row. The algorithm iterates through rows, then within each row, traverses the linked list of non-zero entries, demonstrating how sparse matrix storage enables O(num-rows + num-terms) time complexity for output.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page197_img1.p.caption: This code implements memory deallocation for a sparse matrix stored in a linked list structure, where nodes represent non-zero entries. The algorithm iterates through each row’s linked list, freeing individual entry nodes, then deallocates the row head nodes, and finally the top-level matrix node. The key relationships involve pointer manipulation to traverse the list and `free()` to reclaim memory, demonstrating dynamic memory management in a linked data structure.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page198_img1.p.caption: Doubly linked lists enable bidirectional traversal via left (llink) and right (rlink) pointer fields per node, facilitating efficient insertion and deletion operations. The head node, typically containing no data, serves as a constant reference point for list operations. The relationship `ptr = ptr->llink->rlink = ptr->rlink->llink` demonstrates the structural integrity and bidirectional connectivity that allows for constant-time node insertion when the head node is known.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page199_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates a doubly linked circular list with a head node, a data structure where each node contains `llink` (left link), `item` (data), and `rlink` (right link) fields, forming a circular chain that loops back to the head. The head node acts as a sentinel, simplifying boundary conditions, and the `ptr` in Figure 4.24 indicates the current position in the empty list. The `dinsert` function demonstrates insertion by updating the `llink` and `rlink` pointers of the preceding node and the new node to maintain the circular doubly-linked structure.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page19_img1.pn.caption: The image is too degraded to discern any readable text or diagram. Without a clear visual, I cannot identify the key CS concept, components, or relationships. Please provide a clearer image or describe the content for an accurate technical description.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page200_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates the insertion of a new node into an empty doubly linked circular list, demonstrating the core concept of circular linkage where the tail’s next pointer and head’s previous pointer both reference the same node. The key components are the `newnode` (the inserted element) and the existing `node` (the sole list element), each containing `rlink` (right pointer) and `llink` (left pointer) fields. The critical relationship is that the new node’s `rlink` points to the existing node, and the existing node’s `llink` points to the new node, forming a circular structure with both pointers correctly oriented.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page201_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates the deletion operation in a doubly linked circular list, a linear data structure where each node contains a reference to both its predecessor and successor, forming a circular loop. The key components are the node (containing data and two pointers) and the deleted node, which is removed by updating the predecessor’s next pointer and the successor’s previous pointer to bypass it. The critical relationship is the circular linkage, which ensures traversal continues seamlessly until the head is reached, and the deletion maintains this integrity by preserving pointer consistency.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page202_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates a multilist data structure, a specialized linked-list variant for organizing heterogeneous data. It employs multiple index pointers (Id, Occupation, Location) to map to separate singly linked lists, enabling efficient retrieval by different criteria. Each list is organized by a specific attribute (e.g., row or column), with nodes linked via pointers to maintain structural integrity and facilitate traversal. The design demonstrates how composite data structures can optimize access patterns for complex datasets.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page203_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates an alternate sparse matrix representation using a doubly-linked list structure, where each node stores a non-zero value and its row and column indices. The key components include a shared head node that serves as the root for both row and column lists, with solid lines denoting row links and broken lines denoting column links. The `down` field within each node enables traversal down a column, while the `right` field (implied by row links) allows traversal across a row, forming a compressed, linked-list-based matrix. This structure enables efficient storage and access for sparse matrices by skipping zero elements, with the head node containing matrix dimensions for context.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page204_img1.p.caption: This exercise implements sparse matrix arithmetic using a linked list representation, where non-zero elements are stored as nodes with row, column, and value fields, linked via pointers. Key components include matrix metadata (dimensions), a head pointer to the first row’s list, and traversal logic for row/column access. Operations like addition and multiplication require element-wise comparison and list merging, while transposition involves reassigning row and column indices across linked lists.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page205_img1.p.caption: This figure introduces trees as a hierarchical data structure for modeling relationships, exemplified by genealogical charts. The pedigree chart demonstrates a binary tree structure with two-way branching, while the lineal chart represents a more general tree with potentially multiple descendants per node. Both structures illustrate parent-child relationships, with nodes representing entities and edges representing direct lineage or ancestry.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page206_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates tree structures as recursive, rooted, acyclic graphs used to model hierarchical relationships. Key components include a designated root node and disjoint subtrees, with nodes representing entities (e.g., individuals or languages) and edges denoting ancestry or derivation. The formal definition enforces that every non-root node is the root of exactly one subtree, prohibiting cycles and cross-connections between subtrees.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page207_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates core tree data structure concepts: nodes, edges, and hierarchical relationships. Key components include the root (A), internal nodes (B, D), leaf nodes (E, F, G, H, I, J, K, L, M), and levels indicating depth. Important relationships are defined by parent-child, sibling, ancestor-descendant, and degree (number of children), with the tree’s degree being 3 (maximum node degree).

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page208_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates two key tree data structure representations: a list-based representation and the left-child-right-sibling representation. The list representation uses a node structure with a `data` field and variable `link` fields for children, enabling recursive traversal. The left-child-right-sibling representation uses fixed-size nodes with exactly two pointers: one to the leftmost child and one to the closest right sibling, simplifying memory management and traversal by encoding sibling relationships explicitly.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page209_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates the left-child-right-sibling tree representation, a linked-list-based data structure for general trees. Each node contains three pointers: `data`, `left child` (pointing to the first child), and `right sibling` (pointing to the next sibling). The structure enables efficient traversal of children and siblings using simple pointer navigation, with the root’s right sibling pointer always being null. This representation is foundational for converting arbitrary trees into a binary tree structure, facilitating operations like tree traversal and manipulation.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page20_img1.pn.caption: This figure introduces the system life cycle as the foundational development process for large-scale computer programs, comprising interdependent phases: requirements, analysis, design, coding, and verification. It positions programming beyond syntax mastery, emphasizing system-level thinking through data abstraction, algorithm specification, and performance analysis. The cycle reflects a structured, iterative methodology where each phase informs and constrains the next, enabling systematic construction and validation of complex software systems.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page210_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates the left-child-right-sibling representation, a common tree encoding where each node stores pointers to its leftmost child and its right sibling. The key CS concept is the binary tree abstract data type, defined as a finite set of nodes with a root and two disjoint subtrees (left and right). The critical relationship is the hierarchical parent-child structure, where each node’s degree is at most two, and the left/right distinction is fundamental to binary tree operations. This representation enables efficient traversal and manipulation while preserving the tree’s structural integrity.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page210_img2.p.caption: This figure depicts a binary tree, a non-linear data structure where each node has at most two children (left and right). The root node is 'A', with 'B' and 'C' as its direct children, illustrating a hierarchical parent-child relationship. Nodes 'E' and 'C' are internal nodes with children, while 'K', 'L', 'F', 'G', 'D', 'H', 'M', 'I', and 'J' are leaf nodes (nodes with no children), demonstrating the tree's recursive structure and depth-based organization.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page211_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates core binary tree representations and structural distinctions. It contrasts a general tree (with no node ordering) against a binary tree (where left/right child order matters), showing how the same nodes can form different binary trees based on subtree emptiness. It also introduces two special cases: a skewed tree (where all nodes are left children, creating a linear structure) and a complete binary tree (where all leaf nodes reside on two adjacent levels, optimizing storage and access).

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page212_img1.p.caption: This figure defines the abstract data type `Binary_Tree` using an ADT specification, outlining its structural components (root, left/right subtrees) and core operations (Create, IsEmpty, MakeBT, Lchild, Rchild, Data). The operations enforce structural invariants by checking for emptiness before accessing children or data, ensuring referential integrity. The accompanying diagram illustrates two distinct tree instances, visually reinforcing the recursive, hierarchical nature of binary trees as defined by the ADT.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page212_img3.p.caption: This diagram illustrates a fundamental graph structure, specifically an undirected edge connecting two vertices (A and B). The key components are the nodes (A, B) representing entities and the edge representing a direct relationship or connection between them. This simple graph models binary relationships, forming the basis for more complex networks, and demonstrates adjacency — the property that two nodes are connected by an edge.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page213_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates two binary tree structures: (a) a skewed tree, which is degenerate and linear, and (b) a complete binary tree, where all levels are fully filled except possibly the last, which is filled left-to-right. The accompanying Lemma 5.1 formalizes the maximum node count: level *i* can contain at most 2^(i-1) nodes, and a tree of depth *k* can have at most 2^k - 1 nodes total. The proof uses mathematical induction on level *i*, establishing the base case (level 1 has 1 node) and inductive step (level *i* has twice the nodes of level *i-1*), demonstrating the exponential growth bound inherent in complete binary trees.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page213_img2.p.caption: This figure illustrates two distinct tree structures, each rooted at node A, demonstrating hierarchical organization. The left tree is a linear chain (path) from A to E, while the right tree is a balanced binary tree with nodes B, C, D, E, F, G, H, I. Key relationships include parent-child links (e.g., A is parent of B and C), root nodes (A in both), and leaf nodes (E, F, G, H, I), highlighting how trees model hierarchical data with directed acyclic relationships.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page214_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates key properties of binary trees, specifically the relationship between leaf nodes and nodes of degree two, and the structure of full binary trees. It presents Lemma 5.2, which states that in any nonempty binary tree, the number of leaf nodes (n₀) equals the number of nodes of degree two (n₂) plus one (n₀ = n₂ + 1). The text also defines a full binary tree of depth k as one containing exactly 2ᵏ - 1 nodes, and Figure 5.10 visually demonstrates this structure with a full binary tree of depth 4. The sequential node numbering scheme shown in the figure is foundational for defining complete binary trees.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page215_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates a complete binary tree’s sequential node numbering, enabling efficient array-based representation. Key components include the root (node 2), internal nodes (4, 5), and leaf nodes (8, 9, 10), with nodes numbered level-by-level from left to right. The critical relationships are: parent(i) = floor(i/2), left-child(i) = 2i, and right-child(i) = 2i+1, which allow O(1) access to parent-child relationships in memory.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page215_img2.p.caption: This figure depicts a binary tree, a hierarchical data structure where each node has at most two children (left and right). The root node is 1, with children 3 (its left child) and an implied right child (not shown). Node 3 further branches into left child 6 and right child 7, which in turn have their own children (11, 12, 13, 14, 15), establishing parent-child relationships and defining the tree’s depth and structure.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page216_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates the array-based sequential representation of binary trees, where nodes are stored in a zero-indexed array using level-order traversal. For a complete binary tree, the left child of node at index `i` is at `2i+1` and the right child is at `2i+2`, enabling direct index-based navigation. The skewed tree example demonstrates inefficiency: it wastes space by leaving many array positions unused, requiring `2^k - 1` total slots for depth `k` but only occupying `k` nodes. This representation is optimal for complete trees but suboptimal for skewed or general trees, motivating the use of linked representations.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page217_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates the linked node structure for a binary tree, where each node contains three pointers: `left-child`, `data`, and `right-child`. The `left-child` and `right-child` fields enable hierarchical traversal and tree operations, while the `data` field stores the node’s value. This representation allows dynamic insertion and deletion without shifting elements, contrasting with sequential storage, but lacks direct parent-pointer access, which is addressed in extended node definitions.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page218_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates the linked node structure for binary trees, where each node contains a data field and two pointers (left and right) to its children, with NULL indicating absence of a child. The root node serves as the entry point to the tree, and the hierarchical parent-child relationships define the tree’s structure. Figure 5.14 presents two sample binary trees, demonstrating different topologies that can be represented using the linked structure shown in Figure 5.13.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page218_img2.p.caption: This figure depicts a binary tree, a hierarchical data structure where each node has at most two children. Node B serves as the root, with A and D as its direct children, and C as its left child; C further has E as its left child. The structure illustrates parent-child relationships and levels, with A, D, and E being leaf nodes (nodes with no children).

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page218_img3.p.caption: This figure depicts a binary tree, a hierarchical data structure where each node has at most two children (left and right). The root node is A, with B as its left child and D as its right child; B further branches to C (left) and D (right), illustrating parent-child relationships. Nodes E, F, G, H, and I form the leaves and internal nodes, demonstrating the tree’s recursive structure and depth-based organization.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page219_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates binary tree traversals, a core CS concept for systematically visiting each node exactly once. The key components are the three primary depth-first traversals—**inorder** (LVR), **postorder** (LRV), and **preorder** (VLR)—named by the relative position of the root (V) with respect to left (L) and right (R) subtree visits. These traversals establish a direct correspondence with **infix**, **postfix**, and **prefix** notations of arithmetic expressions, respectively, enabling algorithmic conversion between tree structure and symbolic representation.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page21_img1.pn.caption: This figure outlines the foundational phases of the software development lifecycle: Requirements, Analysis, and Design. The Requirements phase defines system inputs and outputs; Analysis employs top-down or bottom-up strategies to decompose the problem into manageable modules, with top-down being preferred for its structured, diagram-driven approach; and Design focuses on abstract data types and algorithm specification, deferring implementation details to later stages. The key relationships involve progressive refinement from high-level specifications to concrete design elements, with top-down analysis enabling systematic decomposition and design prioritizing abstraction over implementation.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page220_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates two recursive binary tree traversal algorithms: inorder and preorder. Inorder traversal visits nodes by recursively descending left, then printing the current node, then recursively descending right, producing an infix expression (e.g., A/B*C+D+E). Preorder traversal visits the current node first, then recursively processes left and right subtrees, yielding a prefix expression (e.g., +*+A*B*C*D). Both traversals rely on pointer-based tree structure navigation and recursion to systematically explore all nodes.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page221_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates recursive tree traversal, specifically preorder traversal, where a node’s value is processed before recursively visiting its left and right subtrees. The trace shows the call stack, current node value, and action (printf), demonstrating how recursion manages the traversal order through stack frames. The key relationship is that the traversal order is determined by the recursive structure of the binary tree and the sequence of function calls, which directly maps to the prefix (infix) expression evaluation order.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page222_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates postorder tree traversal, a recursive algorithm that processes a binary tree by recursively visiting the left subtree, then the right subtree, and finally printing the root node’s data. The key components are the recursive function `postorder` that operates on a `tree_pointer`, and the implicit stack mechanism that manages node processing order. The traversal’s time complexity is O(n), where n is the number of nodes, as each node is visited exactly once, and the space complexity is O(h), where h is the tree’s height, due to the recursion stack depth.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page223_img1.p.caption: This figure presents an iterative in-order tree traversal algorithm using a stack to simulate recursion, avoiding the overhead of function calls. The algorithm initializes an empty stack, iteratively pushes left children onto the stack, pops nodes to process them, and then traverses right children, ensuring left-to-right visitation order. It demonstrates the core relationship between stack-based control flow and tree structure, where the stack manages the traversal state implicitly. The accompanying text contrasts this with level-order traversal, which requires a queue to process nodes by level, highlighting the fundamental difference in data structure usage for different traversal orders.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page224_img1.p.caption: This code implements a level-order (breadth-first) traversal of a binary tree using a queue data structure. The algorithm processes nodes level by level, starting from the root, by enqueuing the current node, printing its data, and enqueuing its left and right children in that order. The key relationship is between the queue’s FIFO (First-In-First-Out) behavior and the tree’s hierarchical structure, ensuring nodes are visited in order of their depth. The `tree-pointer` type and `addq`/`deleteq` functions manage the queue operations, while the loop condition `(!ptr)` handles the termination condition for an empty tree.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page225_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates a binary tree where each node represents a C language identifier, with parent-child relationships reflecting lexical or syntactic hierarchy. The tree’s structure enables efficient traversal and manipulation, as demonstrated by operations like copying and equality testing that leverage preorder and postorder traversals. Inorder traversal yields alphabetical order because the tree is constructed such that left subtree elements precede the root, which precedes right subtree elements, enforcing lexicographic ordering during traversal.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page226_img1.p.caption: This figure demonstrates recursive tree traversal and structural comparison in binary trees. Program 5.6 implements a depth-first copy operation using pointer dereferencing and recursive calls to clone left and right subtrees, while Program 5.7 defines structural equality via recursive comparison of node data and child subtrees. Both functions rely on pointer semantics and recursion to navigate tree hierarchies, with Program 5.7 using short-circuit evaluation for efficiency.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page227_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates a binary tree representation of a propositional logic formula, demonstrating how logical expressions can be structured hierarchically. The key components include leaf nodes representing atomic propositions (x₁, x₂, x₃) and internal nodes representing logical operators (¬, ∧, ∨), with the tree’s structure enforcing operator precedence via parentheses. The inorder traversal yields the infix expression, and the tree enables efficient evaluation through postorder traversal, which recursively evaluates subtrees before combining results — analogous to postfix evaluation in arithmetic expressions.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page228_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates a binary tree representation of a propositional logic formula, where nodes encode logical operators and operands. The node structure (Figure 5.19) consists of `left-child`, `data` (holding a variable or operator), `value` (holding TRUE/FALSE), and `right-child` fields, enabling recursive evaluation. The tree’s hierarchical structure facilitates bottom-up computation, where a node’s value is derived from its children’s values, leveraging the operator semantics stored in `data`.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page228_img2.p.caption: This figure depicts a binary logic expression tree representing a compound Boolean formula. The root node (¬) denotes negation, with its left child being a disjunction (∨) and right child a negation (¬) applied to variable x₃. The left disjunction (∨) combines a literal x₁ with the negation of x₂ (¬x₂), while the right disjunction (∨) combines the negation of x₃ (¬x₃) with x₁. The tree structure explicitly encodes operator precedence and operand relationships, enabling systematic evaluation of the logical expression.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page229_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates a recursive backtracking algorithm for Boolean satisfiability (SAT) using a binary expression tree. The core concept is to exhaustively enumerate all possible truth assignments (2^n combinations) for n variables, substituting them into the tree, and evaluating the root via postorder traversal to check for satisfiability. Key components include the `node` structure with `left-child`, `data` (logical value), `value`, and `right-child` fields, and the algorithm’s reliance on postorder traversal to correctly evaluate logical expressions rooted at `root`. The relationship between variable assignments and tree evaluation enables the determination of whether any assignment yields a true result, with the algorithm terminating early upon finding a solution.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page22_img1.pn.caption: This figure outlines the refinement and verification phases of the software development lifecycle. Refinement involves selecting data representations and designing algorithms independent of implementation details to ensure efficiency and adaptability, while verification encompasses correctness proofs and testing to validate algorithmic correctness and performance. The key relationship is that early, abstract design decisions in refinement directly impact the feasibility and rigor of subsequent verification efforts, including the need for comprehensive test data and formal methods to ensure correctness and efficiency.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page230_img1.p.caption: This code implements a post-order traversal algorithm for evaluating a binary tree representing propositional calculus expressions. The key components are recursive descent on left and right children, followed by a switch-case evaluation of the node’s operator (not, and, or, true, false) using the computed values of its children. The algorithm leverages the post-order property (children evaluated before parent) to correctly compute logical expressions rooted at internal nodes. The evaluation is performed in-place, modifying the node’s value field to store the result of the subexpression.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page231_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates a binary tree transformation via the `swap_tree()` function, which swaps the left and right subtrees of each node. The key concept is tree restructuring, where the left and right child pointers of every node are inverted, altering the tree’s structure while preserving the node hierarchy. The major components are the root node and its left/right subtrees, with the critical relationship being the bidirectional pointer swap between parent and child nodes. This operation is foundational for understanding tree symmetry and structural manipulation in algorithms.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page232_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates a threaded binary tree, a data structure where null pointers are replaced with threads (pointers) to inorder predecessor or successor nodes, enabling efficient inorder traversal without recursion or a stack. Key components include the `left-thread` and `right-thread` flags indicating threaded pointers, and the `left-child`/`right-child` pointers for actual children. The `root` node serves as both the tree’s head and the starting point for traversal, with its `left-child` pointer pointing to the first node in inorder sequence, resolving the issue of dangling threads. Threads are crucial for maintaining traversal continuity, especially at leaf nodes and the tree’s boundaries.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page233_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates a threaded binary tree node structure, where threads (left_thread, right_thread) replace null pointers to enable efficient in-order traversal without a stack. The key components are the left_child, right_child pointers, and the data field, with threads indicating the inorder predecessor/successor via left_thread=TRUE (for predecessor) or right_thread=TRUE (for successor). The critical relationship is that threads allow direct navigation to inorder successors by following left-child links from a node’s right child until encountering a node with left_thread=TRUE, enabling O(n) traversal with a smaller constant factor than iterative methods.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page234_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates a threaded binary tree, a data structure where null pointers are replaced with threads (pointers) to inorder successors, enabling efficient traversal without recursion or a stack. Each node contains flags ('f' for false, 't' for true) indicating whether its left/right pointer is a thread (pointing to inorder successor) or a regular child pointer. The provided algorithm finds the inorder successor by first checking the right child; if it's a thread, it returns it; otherwise, it traverses left until finding a node whose left child is a thread. This design optimizes inorder traversal time to O(1) per node after initial setup.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page235_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates the insertion of a new node into a threaded binary tree, specifically addressing the case where the parent node has a nonempty right subtree. The key concept is maintaining thread integrity: step (4) updates the child’s right-thread to point to the parent’s original right-child, and step (5) reassigns the parent’s right-child pointer to the new child. This preserves the inorder traversal order by correctly updating the predecessor-successor relationships in the threaded structure.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page236_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates threaded binary tree node insertion, a key data structure technique for optimizing tree traversal without recursion or explicit stacks. The key components are the root node, parent-child relationships, and thread pointers (dotted lines) that link nodes to their inorder predecessors/successors. The “before” state shows the parent’s right child pointer, while the “after” state demonstrates the insertion of a new child node and the reconfiguration of threads to maintain traversal integrity. This operation preserves the threaded tree’s efficiency for inorder traversal by updating only necessary pointers.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page237_img1.p.caption: This figure presents two distinct CS concepts: a threaded binary tree insertion algorithm and heap abstract data types. The `insert-right` function demonstrates threaded tree manipulation by updating parent-child pointers and thread flags to maintain traversal links without explicit null pointers. The heap definitions establish max-heaps (root contains maximum key) and min-heaps (root contains minimum key) as complete binary trees, enabling efficient heap operations via array-based indexing and the heap property.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page238_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates max heap operations: insertion and deletion of the maximum element. The key concept is maintaining the max-heap property (parent ≥ children) during these operations. The major components are the heap structure (represented as a complete binary tree) and the heap-order property ensuring the root holds the maximum value. The important relationship is that deletion requires restoring heap order by percolating the last element up or down, while insertion involves adding to the end and then percolating up to maintain the heap invariant.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page238_img2.p.caption: This figure depicts a binary search tree (BST) with nodes labeled by values and annotated with their respective subtree sizes in square brackets. The key CS concept is BST property: for any node, all values in its left subtree are smaller, and all values in its right subtree are larger. Major components include root (9), internal nodes (6, 7), leaf nodes (3, 5, 14), and edges representing parent-child relationships. The subtree size annotations enable efficient operations like counting nodes or finding k-th smallest element.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page239_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates min-heaps, a specific type of complete binary tree where each node’s value is less than or equal to its children’s values, enforcing a min-heap property. The key components include the heap structure (represented as a tree) and associated ADT functions for creation, insertion, deletion, and checking emptiness or fullness. The critical relationship is that heap operations (like deletion) require searching for the minimum element, which in a binary heap is always at the root, but shifting elements after deletion takes O(n) time due to the tree’s structure.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page23_img1.pn.caption: This figure defines an algorithm as a finite, unambiguous, and terminating sequence of instructions that accepts zero or more inputs and produces at least one output. The key components are input, output, definiteness, finiteness, and effectiveness, which together ensure algorithmic correctness and feasibility. The text distinguishes algorithms from programs by emphasizing that algorithms must terminate, whereas programs may not (e.g., operating system loops), though the text treats them interchangeably for practical purposes.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page240_img1.p.caption: This figure compares priority queue implementations, highlighting that a max heap achieves optimal O(log n) time for both insertion and deletion, outperforming linear structures. The key components are the data structure (array, linked list, heap) and their associated time complexities for insertion and deletion operations. The critical relationship is that heap structure enforces the max-heap property (parent ≥ children), enabling efficient heapify operations that maintain the complete binary tree shape and guarantee logarithmic performance.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page241_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates max-heap insertion, a core data structure operation. The key concept is maintaining the max-heap property (parent ≥ children) through a bottom-up percolation. Major components include the heap array representation, the insertion point (new leaf), and the comparison/swap loop that ascends the tree. The critical relationship is that the loop’s height, O(log n), is determined by the complete binary tree’s depth, ensuring efficient insertion.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page242_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates the deletion operation in a max-heap, which removes the root (maximum) element and restores heap property by percolating the last element down. The key components are the heap array, the deletion function `delete_max_heap`, and the percolation process involving parent-child comparisons and swaps. The operation’s time complexity is O(log₂n) due to the height of the complete binary tree, as the while loop iterates at most log₂n times to re-establish the max-heap invariant.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page243_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates max-heap deletion: the root (maximum element) is removed, replaced with the last leaf, and the heap property is restored via percolation down. Key components include the root node (15), the replacement node (10), and the heap structure’s parent-child relationships. The process maintains the max-heap invariant by comparing and swapping the new root with its larger child until the heap property is satisfied. The final heap (c) shows the restructured tree after deletion and reorganization.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page243_img2.p.caption: This figure depicts a binary search tree (BST) node structure, where each node contains a key and pointers to left and right children. The root node (15) has left child 14 and right child 2, with 14 further having a left child 10. The tree maintains BST ordering: for any node, all keys in its left subtree are smaller, and all keys in its right subtree are larger. The numbered brackets indicate pointer references (e.g., [1] for root, [2] for left child of root, etc.), illustrating the hierarchical parent-child relationships essential for tree traversal and search operations.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page244_img1.p.caption: This code implements the `delete-max-heap` operation for a max-heap data structure, which removes and returns the element with the highest key. The algorithm replaces the root (heap[1]) with the last element in the heap, then restores the max-heap property by repeatedly swapping the parent with the larger of its children until the heap invariant is re-established. Key components include the heap array, the heap size pointer `*n`, and the iterative comparison and swap logic that maintains the heap’s structural and ordering properties. The operation relies on the heap’s complete binary tree representation and the heap property (parent ≥ children), ensuring O(log n) time complexity.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page245_img1.p.caption: A binary search tree (BST) is a specialized binary tree data structure where each node’s key is greater than all keys in its left subtree and less than all keys in its right subtree, ensuring ordered structure. Key components include nodes (each storing a key and pointers to left and right children) and the root node, which anchors the tree hierarchy. The BST property enables efficient search, insertion, and deletion operations in O(h) time, where h is the tree height, though worst-case performance degrades to O(n) for unbalanced trees. The recursive nature of the BST definition implies that both subtrees must also be valid BSTs, enforcing a self-consistent ordering invariant across all levels.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page246_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates the recursive search algorithm for a Binary Search Tree (BST), a data structure where each node’s key is greater than all keys in its left subtree and less than all keys in its right subtree. The search begins at the root and recursively traverses left (if key < current node) or right (if key > current node) based on the comparison, terminating when the key is found or a null pointer is reached. The recursive function `search` leverages the BST property to guarantee logarithmic time complexity in balanced trees, with the key relationship being: for any node, all keys in the left subtree are < node’s key, and all keys in the right subtree are > node’s key.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page247_img1.p.caption: This figure demonstrates iterative search in a binary search tree (BST) using a while loop to replace recursion, maintaining O(h) time complexity where h is the tree height. The algorithm traverses left or right based on key comparisons, terminating when the target is found or a null child is reached. It highlights the BST invariant: for any node, left subtree keys are smaller and right subtree keys are larger, enabling efficient search via pointer navigation. The iterative approach eliminates O(h) stack space overhead compared to the recursive `search` function.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page248_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates the insertion operation in a binary search tree (BST), a dynamic data structure maintaining the BST property: for any node, all values in its left subtree are less than the node’s value, and all values in its right subtree are greater. The key components are the root node (30), left and right child pointers, and the insertion algorithm that traverses the tree by comparing the target value with node values to determine the insertion path. The relationship is hierarchical and ordered: insertion maintains the BST invariant by placing new nodes in the correct subtree (left if smaller, right if larger), ensuring efficient search, insertion, and deletion operations with O(log n) average-case complexity.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page249_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates deletion in a binary search tree (BST), a core data structure operation. The key concept is maintaining BST invariants by replacing a node with either its in-order predecessor (largest in left subtree) or successor (smallest in right subtree) when deleting a non-leaf node with two children. The major components are the target node, its children, and the replacement node, with the critical relationship being that the replacement node must preserve the BST property (left ≤ node < right) and the deletion operation must be performed in O(h) time, where h is the tree’s height.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page249_img2.p.caption: This figure depicts a binary tree, a hierarchical data structure where each node has at most two children. The root node is 30, with left child 5 and right child 80; node 5 further has a left child 2, forming a left-skewed subtree. The parent-child relationships define the tree’s structure, and the values suggest a potential binary search tree property (left child < parent < right child), though not strictly enforced here.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page24_img1.pn.caption: This figure illustrates the Selection Sort algorithm, a comparison-based sorting technique that operates by repeatedly identifying the minimum element from the unsorted portion and swapping it with the first unsorted element. The core components include an iterative loop that partitions the array and a swap operation, which can be implemented via either a function or a macro. The algorithm’s efficiency is O(n²) due to nested comparisons, and its key relationship lies in the sequential reduction of the unsorted segment through each iteration.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page250_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates the structural transformation of a binary search tree during node deletion, specifically when the target node has two children. The deletion process requires replacing the node with its in-order predecessor or successor (not shown) to maintain the BST property, resulting in the depicted tree structure after removal. The text emphasizes that unbalanced trees can degrade to O(n) height, motivating the use of balanced search trees like AVL or red-black trees to guarantee O(log n) operations. The figure serves as a visual example of the dynamic nature of BSTs and the necessity of rebalancing for optimal performance.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page250_img2.p.caption: This figure illustrates a binary search tree (BST) undergoing a node replacement operation, likely during deletion. The left tree shows a node with value 60 (highlighted in black) being replaced by its in-order successor, 55, which is then shaded to indicate it is being promoted. The right tree depicts the resulting structure after the replacement, where the successor (55) takes the place of the deleted node (60), and the original successor's parent (50) now points to its former left child (45). This maintains the BST invariant: for any node, all values in its left subtree are smaller, and all values in its right subtree are larger.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page251_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates a selection tree, a specialized binary tree data structure used for efficiently merging k sorted sequences (runs). Each nonleaf node stores the minimum key among its children, enabling O(1) access to the global minimum, while leaf nodes point to the first record of their respective runs. The tree’s structure mirrors a tournament, where the root identifies the overall minimum, and subsequent comparisons propagate along the path to the winner, facilitating optimal k-way merge operations with O(log k) per extraction.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page252_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates a selection tree, a tournament-based data structure used for efficiently selecting the minimum (or maximum) element from multiple sorted runs. The tree’s key components are internal nodes representing tournament winners and leaf nodes representing sorted runs; sibling nodes compete in a tournament to determine the next smallest key. The algorithm maintains O(log k) restructuring time per output, leading to an overall O(n log k) time complexity for merging k sorted runs, leveraging the hierarchical comparison structure to minimize redundant scans.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page253_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates a tournament tree restructured after one record is output, demonstrating a “tree of losers” data structure. Each nonleaf node points to the loser of its subtournament, enabling efficient record retrieval and tournament reorganization. The leaf nodes represent initial records, and the tree is restructured by replaying matches along the path from the winner (node 11) to the root (node 1), leveraging parent pointers for quick access to required records.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page254_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates a selection tree, specifically a “tree of losers,” used in tournament-style selection algorithms. The tree’s structure is a complete binary tree where internal nodes represent match outcomes (winners), and leaf nodes represent original records (runs) with their associated run numbers. The root node (value 8) points to the overall winner, while each internal node’s left and right children represent the two competitors in that match, with the winner advancing upward. The tree’s construction enables efficient selection of the maximum (or minimum) element across multiple sorted runs, with the overall winner being the root node.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page255_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates a forest—a set of disjoint trees—consisting of three individual binary trees. The key components are the root nodes (E, G, and I), each forming a separate tree with their respective subtrees. The transformation into a binary tree involves converting each tree into its binary representation and then linking them via sibling pointers, establishing a hierarchical relationship among the original roots. This structure enables efficient forest traversal and set operations by unifying multiple trees into a single hierarchical entity.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page256_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates the binary tree representation of a forest, a data structure where each tree in the forest is encoded as a node in a binary tree. The key concept is the bijection between forest traversals and binary tree traversals: preorder traversal of the binary tree corresponds to tree preorder of the forest, while inorder traversal of the binary tree corresponds to tree inorder of the forest. The binary tree’s root represents the root of the first tree in the forest, its left subtree encodes the children of that root, and its right subtree encodes the remaining trees in the forest. This encoding enables recursive traversal algorithms to process forests using standard binary tree traversal techniques.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page256_img2.p.caption: This figure depicts a binary tree, a hierarchical data structure where each node has at most two children (left and right). The root node is A, with subtrees rooted at B (left) and E (right); E further branches to F (left) and G (right), and G branches to H, which in turn branches to I. Nodes B, F, and I are leaf nodes (no children), while A, E, and G are internal nodes with children, illustrating parent-child relationships and tree traversal paths.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page257_img1.p.caption: This section introduces forest traversal algorithms and set representation using trees. The key concept is defining postorder traversal for a forest (a collection of trees) by recursively processing each subtree before visiting the root, mirroring binary tree postorder traversal. The major components are the forest structure and the recursive traversal algorithm, with the critical relationship being that forest postorder traversal visits children before the root, unlike preorder which visits the root first. This representation enables efficient set operations by linking children to parents, facilitating union, intersection, and difference through tree manipulation.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page258_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates the disjoint-set data structure, specifically the union and find operations used to manage and query connected components. The forest representation (Figure 5.39) depicts each set as a tree, where nodes represent elements and the root denotes the set representative. The union operation (Figure 5.40) merges two trees by attaching one root as a child of the other, maintaining the forest structure while preserving the set’s identity through the root. The find operation locates the root of a given element’s tree, enabling efficient component identification via path compression or union by rank optimizations.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page258_img8.p.caption: This diagram illustrates a **trie (prefix tree)**, a tree-like data structure optimized for storing associative arrays where keys are strings. The nodes represent characters or prefixes, with edges indicating character transitions; the root (0) represents the empty prefix. The dashed arrow from node 4 to node 0 indicates a **failure link** (or failure pointer), used in algorithms like the Aho-Corasick string matching automaton to handle mismatches efficiently. The structure enables efficient insertion, deletion, and prefix-based search operations.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page258_img9.p.caption: This diagram illustrates a directed acyclic graph (DAG) representing a partial order, where nodes denote elements and directed edges denote a "less than or equal to" relationship. The key components include the minimal element (0), maximal elements (4 and 9), and the partial ordering defined by the transitive closure of the directed edges. The relationships show that 0 is less than or equal to 4 and 9, and 6, 7, and 8 are all less than or equal to 0, forming a Hasse diagram structure.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page259_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates a forest of disjoint-set trees representing three sets, S₁, S₂, and S₃, where each set is identified by its root node. The key components are the set name table (mapping element indices to set names) and the parent pointer array, which stores the parent of each node (−1 for roots). The critical relationship is that following parent pointers from any element leads to its set’s root, enabling efficient set identification via the union-find operations.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page25_img1.pn.caption: This figure illustrates the `swap` function, a fundamental operation in sorting algorithms that exchanges the values of two integer variables via pointer dereferencing. The function uses a temporary variable to facilitate the exchange by first storing the value pointed to by `*y`, then overwriting `*x` with that value, and finally restoring `*y` with the original value of `*x`. This demonstrates pointer arithmetic and memory manipulation, essential for in-place data rearrangement. The context implies this function is a subroutine within a larger sorting algorithm, likely selection sort, which iteratively finds and swaps minimum elements to achieve sorted order.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page260_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates the array-based representation of a disjoint-set forest, where each element’s parent pointer indicates its set membership. The key components are the `parent` array, where a negative value at index `i` denotes the root of set `S_i`, and non-negative values point to the parent of that element. The `find` operation traverses parent pointers to locate the root, while the `union` operation merges two sets by setting one root’s parent to the other. These operations form the basis of the union-find data structure, enabling efficient dynamic connectivity queries and set manipulations.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page261_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates a degenerate tree—a linear chain structure resulting from naive union operations—where each node points to its immediate successor, leading to O(n²) total time complexity for n-1 find operations due to the cumulative path lengths. The key concept is that such degeneracy undermines the efficiency of union-find data structures, motivating the use of the weighting rule, which maintains a count field at each root to ensure the smaller tree is attached under the larger one. This rule guarantees that the height of any resulting tree remains logarithmic (≤ ⌊log₂n⌋ + 1), ensuring O(log n) amortized time per operation. The relationship between tree structure and operation cost is critical: minimizing height directly optimizes the performance of find operations.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page262_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates the Union-Find data structure’s tree representation under the weighting rule, which optimizes union operations by always attaching the smaller tree to the root of the larger tree to maintain balance. The key components are the parent array (tracking root pointers) and the count array (storing subtree sizes), with the union operation updating these to enforce the weighting rule. The relationship between the union operation and tree height is critical: the weighting rule ensures the maximum tree depth remains logarithmic (O(log n)), guaranteeing efficient find operations.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page263_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates the union-find data structure’s performance improvement via the collapsing rule. The key concept is path compression, where during a find operation, each node along the path to the root is made a direct child of the root, reducing future find times. This optimization, implemented in find2, transforms the worst-case time complexity from O(n) to nearly constant amortized time, leveraging the inverse Ackermann function α(m,n) to bound the total cost of a sequence of unions and finds.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page264_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates the worst-case performance of Union-Find data structures using disjoint-set forests, where each tree represents a set and parent pointers denote the union operation. The key components are nodes (elements), parent pointers (for tree structure), and the union operation that merges sets by attaching smaller trees to larger ones. The critical relationship shown is how the height of trees grows logarithmically with the number of elements, bounded by Ackermann’s function, which is used to define the minimum height required to achieve the worst-case bound.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page265_img1.p.caption: This figure presents a union-find data structure’s `find2` function implementing path compression via the collapsing rule, which flattens the tree by setting each node along the path from a queried element to the root as direct children of the root. The algorithm iteratively traverses from the queried node `i` to the root, updating parent pointers to point directly to the root, thereby amortizing future `find` operations. The accompanying text discusses the theoretical complexity bounds involving the Ackermann function `A(m,n)` and Tarjan’s lemma, which establishes that the amortized time for `m` finds and `n` unions is nearly linear, specifically `O(α(m,n))`, where `α(m,n)` is the inverse Ackermann function — a very slowly growing function.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page266_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates the application of union-find data structures to manage equivalence classes, where each tree in the forest represents a disjoint set. The key components are the parent array (initially -1 for singleton sets) and the union-find operations (find and union) that dynamically merge equivalence classes. The relationship is that each equivalence pair triggers a find to locate root representatives and a union to merge their sets, maintaining the forest’s structure to track connectivity.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page267_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates the Union-Find data structure’s tree-based representation for managing disjoint sets, where each node points to its parent and the path compression technique reduces future lookup costs. The key components are nodes (representing elements) and edges (representing set membership), with the root node indicating the set representative. The equivalence relations (e.g., 0 ≡ 4, 3 ≡ 1) define set unions, and the tree structure evolves to reflect these relationships, with path compression flattening the hierarchy for efficiency. Figure 5.47 complements this by showing all possible binary tree structures for n=2, highlighting combinatorial enumeration in tree theory.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page268_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates the enumeration of structurally distinct binary trees with exactly three nodes, showcasing all possible non-isomorphic tree topologies. Each tree is represented by its unique parenthetical structure, encoding the left and right child relationships recursively. The figure serves as a foundational example for understanding Catalan numbers, which count the number of such trees (C₃ = 5), and establishes the basis for analyzing tree traversals and combinatorial properties in binary tree algorithms.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page269_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates the reconstruction of a binary tree from its inorder and preorder traversal sequences, a core algorithmic technique in tree data structures. The preorder sequence (root, left, right) identifies the root first, while the inorder sequence (left, root, right) determines the relative positions of left and right subtrees. The key relationship is that the first element in the preorder sequence is always the root, and its position in the inorder sequence partitions the tree into left and right subtrees, enabling recursive reconstruction.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page269_img2.p.caption: This figure depicts a binary tree, a hierarchical data structure where each node has at most two children (left and right). The root node is A, with subtrees rooted at B (left child of A) and D (right child of A). Key relationships include parent-child links (e.g., A is parent of B and D), sibling relationships (B and C are siblings under A), and the tree’s depth (4 levels from root to leaf I). The structure enables efficient operations like search, insertion, and deletion via recursive traversal.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page269_img3.p.caption: This figure depicts a binary tree, a hierarchical data structure where each node has at most two children (left and right). The root node is 1, with left subtree rooted at 2 and right subtree rooted at 4; node 6 is the right child of 4, and node 7 is its left child. Parent-child relationships define the tree’s structure, with edges indicating direct connections, and nodes 3, 5, 8, and 9 being leaf nodes (nodes with no children).

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page26_img1.pn.caption: This code implements the selection sort algorithm, a comparison-based sorting technique with O(n²) time complexity. The algorithm partitions the array into a sorted and unsorted region, repeatedly finding the minimum element from the unsorted portion and swapping it to the sorted region's end. Key components include the `sort` function that uses nested loops to locate the minimum index, and a macro `SWAP` for efficient in-place element exchange. The program demonstrates input validation, random data generation, and output formatting within a C procedural context.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page270_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates the bijection between binary tree structures and inorder permutations, demonstrating that each distinct binary tree with n nodes corresponds to a unique inorder traversal permutation of the node labels 1 through n. The five possible permutations of the sequence (1, 2, 3) generated via stack operations directly map to the five structurally distinct binary trees, establishing a combinatorial equivalence. This relationship is foundational for deriving the Catalan number, which counts the number of such trees. The figure visually confirms that the number of distinct binary trees with n nodes equals the number of valid stack permutations of n elements.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page271_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates the combinatorial equivalence between the number of ways to parenthesize matrix chains, the number of binary trees with n nodes, and the number of stack permutations of n+1 elements. The key concept is dynamic programming via recursive decomposition: the total count \( b_n \) is the sum over all possible root splits into subtrees with \( b_i \) and \( b_{n-i-1} \) nodes, establishing a recurrence relation. This relationship is foundational for understanding Catalan numbers and their applications in algorithm design, particularly in optimal matrix chain multiplication.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page272_img1.p.caption: This figure derives the closed-form expression for the number of distinct binary trees with $n$ nodes using generating functions. It defines the ordinary generating function $B(x) = \sum_{i \geq 0} b_i x^i$, where $b_n$ counts the trees, and derives the functional equation $xB^2(x) = B(x) - 1$ from the recurrence. Solving this quadratic yields $B(x) = \frac{1 - \sqrt{1 - 4x}}{2x}$, which is expanded via the binomial theorem to extract the coefficient $b_n = \frac{1}{n+1} \binom{2n}{n}$, establishing the Catalan number relationship.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page273_img1.p.caption: This figure introduces a k-ary tree of height h, where all nodes have the same size, and poses two analytical questions: (a) determining the maximum number of nodes possible under these constraints, and (b) calculating the total number of NULL pointers in the tree’s structure. The key concept is the structural relationship between tree height, node degree (k), and node count, which directly determines pointer usage. The maximum node count is achieved when the tree is complete, and the number of NULL pointers equals the total number of child slots minus the actual number of children, which is a function of the tree’s structure and degree.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page274_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates a hybrid tree representation using a linked list of nodes, where each node contains a `tag` field (TRUE/FALSE) to distinguish between link nodes (internal) and data nodes (leaf), along with `dlink/data` and `link` pointers. The structure enables efficient traversal and manipulation of the tree via a linear list, with `TRUE` nodes acting as internal links pointing to child subtrees and `FALSE` nodes holding data values. The root node initiates the list, and `NULL` pointers denote the absence of children or the end of a subtree, maintaining the hierarchical relationships through explicit pointer chains.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page274_img2.p.caption: This diagram depicts a directed acyclic graph (DAG) representing a hierarchical dependency structure, commonly used in task scheduling or data flow analysis. The root node 'A' branches into children 'B' and 'C', with 'B' further branching into 'E' and 'F', and 'E' branching into 'H' and 'I', which in turn branches into 'J' and 'K'. Nodes 'C' and 'D' are direct children of 'A' with no further descendants, indicating terminal or leaf nodes in this dependency graph. The directed edges signify dependencies: a node can only be processed after all its parent nodes are resolved, enforcing a topological ordering for execution or evaluation.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page275_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates a cross-reference generator project that processes text to produce an alphabetized report of word occurrences. The key components include a word-frequency counter, a line-number tracker, and a case-insensitive string comparator. The system maps each unique word to its count and the set of line numbers where it appears, enabling efficient retrieval and reporting. The output format enforces a consistent, structured presentation of lexical analysis results.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page276_img1.p.caption: This figure introduces graphs as an abstract data type through the historical Koenigsberg bridge problem, modeling land areas as vertices and bridges as edges. The core components are a set of vertices (A–D) and a set of edges (a–g) connecting them, forming an undirected, unweighted graph. The problem seeks an Euler circuit—a closed walk traversing each edge exactly once—illustrating the fundamental relationship between graph structure and path existence.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page277_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates the Koenigsberg bridge problem, a foundational application of graph theory. It depicts a multigraph where landmasses (vertices A, B, C, D) are connected by bridges (edges a–g), demonstrating Euler’s analysis of vertex degrees. The key insight is that an Eulerian circuit (returning to start) exists only if all vertices have even degree, which fails here due to all four vertices having odd degrees. This problem established the criteria for Eulerian paths and circuits, underpinning graph theory’s use in network analysis, routing, and circuit design.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page278_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates fundamental graph theory concepts: an undirected graph (G₁) with bidirectional edges, a tree (G₂) — an acyclic connected graph — and a directed graph (G₃) with ordered, unidirectional edges. The key components are vertices (V) and edges (E), represented as sets, with G₁ and G₂ being connected and acyclic (G₂ is a tree), while G₃ exhibits directed edges with a clear tail-to-head relationship. The visual distinction between undirected lines and directed arrows highlights the core difference in edge semantics, which impacts traversal and algorithmic behavior.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page278_img2.p.caption: This figure depicts a binary tree, a hierarchical data structure where each node has at most two children. Node 0 is the root, with left child 1 and right child 2; node 1 has left child 3 and right child 4; node 2 has right child 5. The parent-child relationship defines the tree’s structure, enabling operations like traversal and search, while the absence of right children in nodes 3 and 4 indicates they are leaf nodes.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page279_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates fundamental graph constraints: (a) a simple graph with a self-loop (an edge from a vertex to itself, which is disallowed in simple graphs) and (b) a multigraph with multiple parallel edges between vertices (which violates the simple graph’s edge uniqueness constraint). The text defines adjacency (vertices connected by an edge) and incidence (an edge connecting two vertices), and introduces the concept of a subgraph as a graph whose vertex and edge sets are subsets of the original graph’s.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page279_img2.p.caption: This diagram depicts a finite automaton with two states (0 and 1), where state 0 is the initial state (indicated by the incoming arrow from nowhere) and both states have self-loops, suggesting they can be revisited. State 1 has a transition to state 0, forming a cycle between the two states. The self-loop on state 0 implies it can process input symbols without changing state, while the self-loop on state 1 indicates it can also remain in that state upon receiving input. This structure represents a simple deterministic finite automaton (DFA) capable of recognizing a regular language defined by the transitions between these two states.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page27_img1.pn.caption: This figure illustrates the core logic of binary search on a sorted array, using divide-and-conquer to efficiently locate a target value. The algorithm maintains two pointers, `left` and `right`, which define the current search range; the middle index is calculated as `(left + right) / 2` to partition the array. Based on the comparison of `searchnum` with `list[middle]`, the search space is halved by adjusting either `left` or `right`, ensuring logarithmic time complexity. The search terminates when the target is found or the pointers cross, indicating the absence of the target.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page280_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates fundamental graph theory concepts through subgraphs of two directed graphs, G₁ and G₃. It defines a path as a sequence of vertices connected by edges, distinguishing simple paths (no repeated vertices except start/end) from cycles (simple paths where start and end vertices are identical). The text further clarifies that connectivity in undirected graphs requires a path between every pair of vertices, contrasting connected graphs (G₁, G₂) with disconnected ones (G₄).

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page281_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates fundamental graph theory concepts: Figure 6.5 depicts an undirected graph with two connected components (H₁ and H₂), where each component is a maximal connected subgraph. Figure 6.6 shows the strongly connected components of a directed graph G₃, where each component is a maximal subgraph in which every vertex is reachable from every other vertex. The text defines vertex degree as the count of incident edges, and for undirected graphs, the total number of edges is half the sum of all vertex degrees.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page282_img1.p.caption: This figure defines the Graph Abstract Data Type (ADT) as an undirected graph, specifying core operations for vertex and edge manipulation, including creation, insertion, deletion, and adjacency queries. The ADT’s key components are a set of vertices and undirected edges, with operations ensuring structural integrity (e.g., deleting a vertex removes all incident edges). The specification lays groundwork for subsequent graph algorithms (like traversal and property detection) and introduces three primary representations: adjacency matrices, lists, and multilists.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page283_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates adjacency matrices for three graphs, demonstrating how graphs are represented as matrices where entry `adj-mat[i][j] = 1` iff there is an edge from vertex `i` to vertex `j`. The matrices reveal key structural properties: undirected graphs yield symmetric matrices (e.g., G₁, G₃), while directed graphs (e.g., G₄) may be asymmetric. The row sum for a vertex in an undirected graph gives its degree, while in a directed graph, the row sum is the out-degree and the column sum is the in-degree. These representations enable efficient edge existence queries but can be space-inefficient for sparse graphs, motivating alternative storage strategies.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page284_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates the adjacency list data structure, a linked-list-based representation of graphs that replaces the adjacency matrix for improved efficiency. Each vertex maintains a linked list of its adjacent vertices, enabling O(n + e) time complexity for degree calculation and edge enumeration. The structure requires n head nodes and 2e list nodes for undirected graphs, and inverse adjacency lists are needed to efficiently compute in-degrees in directed graphs.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page285_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates adjacency lists, a fundamental graph data structure where each vertex maintains a linked list of its adjacent vertices. The key components are head nodes (indexed by vertex ID) and edge nodes, each containing a vertex link field pointing to the next adjacent vertex or NULL. The structure enables efficient traversal of neighbors and supports sparse graph representation, with vertices ordered arbitrarily as the figure notes.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page285_img2.p.caption: This diagram illustrates a linked list data structure, where each node contains a data field and a pointer to the next node. The top portion shows a singly linked list with nodes storing values (1, 0, 0, 0) and forward-pointing links, while the bottom portion depicts an empty list with null pointers. The key relationship is that nodes are dynamically allocated and chained together via pointers, enabling sequential access and flexible memory management.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page286_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates three graph representations: a sequential array-based storage, an inverse adjacency list, and an alternate node structure for adjacency lists. The sequential representation (Fig. 6.9) stores vertex data in contiguous memory, while the inverse adjacency list (Fig. 6.10) uses linked nodes to track incoming edges. The alternate node structure (Fig. 6.11) employs shared nodes via tail/head and column/row links to optimize edge traversal in multilists, where each edge is represented by a single node shared across two adjacency lists.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page287_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates three graph representations: an orthogonal representation (Fig. 6.12) using linked lists for adjacency, an alternate adjacency list (Fig. 6.13) with vertex links, and a node structure for adjacency multilists (Fig. 6.14). The orthogonal representation uses headnodes to index vertex lists, while the adjacency list uses vertex links to traverse incident vertices. The multilist node structure (Fig. 6.14) combines vertex and path information, enabling efficient traversal of both vertices and edges in a graph.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page288_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates an adjacency multilist representation of an undirected graph G₁, where each vertex maintains a linked list of its adjacent vertices via edge-pointer structures. The key components include a `struct edge` containing `vertex1`, `vertex2`, and `path1/path2` pointers for bidirectional traversal, alongside a global `edge-pointer graph[MAX_VERTICES]` array serving as the headnode table.
