The table demonstrates that algorithmic efficiency is highly dependent on input characteristics, with Insert Sort performing poorly on worst-case inputs and Quick Sort being sensitive to pivot selection. The relationships highlight the trade-offs between average-case efficiency and worst-case guarantees, with Merge Sort offering the most predictable performance.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page389_img1.p.caption: This plot illustrates the average-case time complexity of four comparison-based sorting algorithms—insert sort, heap sort, merge sort, and quick sort—as a function of input size n. The graph demonstrates that merge sort and heap sort maintain consistent, predictable performance (O(n log n)), while insert sort degrades to O(n²) and quick sort exhibits near-linear behavior on average (O(n log n)) but with higher variance. The key relationship is that for sufficiently large n, merge sort and heap sort outperform quick sort, which in turn outperforms insert sort, reflecting their respective asymptotic complexities and practical efficiency trade-offs.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page38_img1.pn.caption: This figure introduces space complexity, a computational analysis metric quantifying memory usage. It decomposes total space into fixed requirements (e.g., code, constants) and variable requirements (dependent on input size, e.g., array storage, recursion stack). The total space is modeled as S(P) = c + S_p(I), where c is fixed and S_p(I) is the variable component expressed as a function of input characteristics I. The text emphasizes that variable space is often the primary focus for comparative analysis.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page390_img1.p.caption: This code implements a Fisher-Yates shuffle algorithm to generate a random permutation of an array in-place. It iterates backward from the last element, selecting a random index `j` from the unshuffled portion `[0, i]` and swapping `list[j]` with `list[i]`, ensuring uniform randomness. The algorithm operates in O(n) time and uses O(1) auxiliary space, making it efficient for generating random permutations for empirical sorting algorithm analysis.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page391_img1.p.caption: This figure outlines an experimental comparison of internal sorting algorithms—Insertion Sort, Heap Sort, Quicksort, Merge Sort, and Radix Sort—across distinct input data patterns (Ordered, Nearly ordered, Reverse ordered, Random) to quantify their comparative performance in terms of comparisons and exchanges. The key components are the algorithm implementations, the input data generators, and the performance metrics (comparisons/exchanges) collected across varying input sizes (50 to 5000). The relationships highlight how algorithmic efficiency varies with data distribution, with algorithms like Quicksort exhibiting worst-case degradation on reverse-ordered data and Merge Sort maintaining consistent performance regardless of input pattern.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page392_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates the **blocked runs** produced by an **external sorting** phase using **merge sort**. The key concept is **external memory management**, where data is partitioned into sorted **runs** (here, six runs of 750 records each) that fit within the system’s **internal memory** (250 records per block). The **major components** are the **input buffers** (for reading runs) and **output buffer** (for writing merged runs), with **important relationships** defined by the **merge tree pattern** and **block boundaries** that dictate how runs are combined and written to disk.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page393_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates the final merge pass of an external sorting algorithm, where six initial sorted runs (each 750 records) are successively merged into progressively larger sorted runs (1500, then 3000, then 4500 records) to produce the final sorted output. The key components are the input runs, merge buffers, and the output run, with the process governed by merge operations that combine sorted subsequences. The time complexity is dominated by the number of passes and disk I/O operations, as characterized by parameters like maximum seek time, latency, and merge time per block.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page394_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates the time complexity of a multi-pass external sorting algorithm, detailing the cost of reading, internally sorting, and merging runs of data across disk I/O and CPU operations. The key components are the initial pass (reading 18 blocks, internally sorting, and writing 18 runs), followed by two merge passes: first merging 6 runs of 1500 records into 3 runs, then merging those into a single run of 3000 records. The total time is dominated by I/O operations (132t<sub>IO</sub> + 12,000t<sub>m</sub> + 6t<sub>IS</sub>), highlighting the 2-3 pass merging strategy’s efficiency and the critical role of buffer management in minimizing CPU idle time. The analysis underscores that parallelism between I/O and CPU processing can significantly reduce total runtime in non-multi-programming environments.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page395_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates k-way merging, a sorting optimization that reduces the number of passes over data by simultaneously merging k sorted runs instead of two. The key components are k input runs (S₁, S₂, ..., Sₖ) and a merge process that selects the smallest key across all k runs for output. The relationship is that increasing k reduces I/O passes from log₂m to logₖm, but increases CPU cost due to k-1 key comparisons per output record.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page396_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates a k-way merge tree, a data structure used in external sorting to efficiently merge sorted runs. The tree has k leaves, each representing an input run, and a height of ⌈logₖ m⌉ + 1 levels, where m is the number of runs. The key relationship is that the total internal processing time is O(n log₂ k logₖ m), which simplifies to O(n log₂ m) for large k, making the time complexity independent of k. This structure enables a reduction in the number of passes over disk, though it increases buffer requirements and may reduce block size, impacting I/O performance.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page396_img2.p.caption: This figure illustrates a **binary tree**, a hierarchical data structure where each node has at most two children—left and right. The structure enforces a parent-child relationship, with the topmost node being the **root** and nodes with no children being **leaves**. The branching pattern visually represents the recursive nature of tree traversal and operations like insertion, deletion, and search, which rely on comparing node values to navigate the tree.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page396_img3.p.caption: This diagram illustrates the structure of a **complete k-ary tree** with **m** total nodes, where the root node is at level 0. The key concept is that the **last level** (level ℓ) contains nodes from index `⌊m/k⌋k + 1` to `m`, which may be incomplete. The relationship between the total number of nodes `m` and the tree’s height `ℓ` is governed by the formula `m ≤ k^(ℓ+1) - 1`, ensuring the tree is as full as possible. The diagram visually represents the **level-order traversal** of the tree’s final level, highlighting the **parent-child relationships** and the **maximum node count** for a given height.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page397_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates buffer management in a k-way merge sort, emphasizing the need for 2k input buffers to prevent pipeline stalls during parallel merging. The key components are k input buffers (one per run), one output buffer, and internal buffers for temporary storage; the critical relationship is that buffer assignment must prioritize the run whose buffer is empty to avoid blocking. The algorithm ensures continuous processing by dynamically assigning buffers based on which run’s buffer is exhausted first, maintaining optimal throughput.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page398_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates the limitation of using only two fixed buffers per run in external sorting, which prevents sustained parallelism. The diagram depicts a multi-stage merge process where input runs (in[1], in[2], etc.) are merged into output buffers (ou[1], ou[2]), but buffer exhaustion forces sequential I/O, halting parallel processing. The key relationship is that insufficient buffer allocation creates a bottleneck, forcing the system to wait for I/O completion before proceeding, thereby reducing overall throughput and efficiency.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page399_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates the merge phase of external sorting, where sorted runs from multiple input buffers (m[1], m[2], m[3]) are merged into fewer output buffers (ou[1], ou[2]). The process demonstrates a two-way merge, with each output buffer receiving sorted data from two input buffers, and the merge operation maintaining sorted order by comparing and outputting the smallest available element. The recursive nature of the merge is evident as the input buffers themselves are the outputs of prior merge steps, forming a merge tree structure.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page39_img1.pn.caption: This figure contrasts iterative and recursive function implementations for summing an array, highlighting space complexity trade-offs. The iterative version (Program 1.10) uses constant stack space, while the recursive version (Program 1.11) incurs linear space overhead proportional to the input size due to stack frame accumulation. The recursive call stack stores parameters, local variables, and return addresses for each invocation, leading to O(n) space complexity versus O(1) for the iterative counterpart. This exemplifies the critical trade-off between algorithmic elegance and memory efficiency in imperative programming.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page3_img1.png.caption: The image is blank and contains no visual content or textual information. Therefore, it is not possible to describe any key computer science concept, components, or relationships based on the provided figure.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page400_img1.p.caption: This figure describes a k-way merge algorithm for external sorting, leveraging parallel I/O to optimize performance. Key components include k input buffers (one per run), 2k output buffers, and a linked stack for managing empty buffers, with sentinel records ensuring consistent run boundaries. The algorithm assumes equal key handling prioritizes the run with the smallest index, and buffer management is optimized under constraints of simultaneous I/O and memory access serialization.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page401_img1.p.caption: This figure details the buffering algorithm for k-way external merge sort, a core technique for sorting data that exceeds main memory. It employs k input buffers (queues) and a linked stack of free blocks to manage data flow, using a k-way merge function (`kwaymerge`) to combine sorted runs. The algorithm advances by selecting the run with the minimum last key (`lastkey`) to feed into the merge, and it handles concurrent I/O by initiating new input reads only after current writes complete, minimizing idle time. The key relationship is that the algorithm’s efficiency hinges on overlapping I/O with computation, with the only non-overlapping phases being the initial input and final output.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page402_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates the initial state of three sorted runs in an external sorting algorithm, each consisting of four blocks of two records. The runs are represented as queues of blocks, with sentinel values (+∞) marking the end of each run to facilitate merging. The key components are the input buffer queues (one per run) and output buffers, which manage data flow during the k-way merge process. The relationships are defined by the block-by-block comparison and merging logic, where the smallest key from the active input buffers is selected for output, maintaining sorted order across the entire dataset.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page403_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates external sorting using a multi-pass merge strategy with a line queue buffer. Key components include input runs (Run 1, Run 2, Run 3), a line queue for temporary storage, and output blocks that are merged in ascending order. The process demonstrates how sorted runs are progressively merged by reading from the line queue and writing to output, with pointers indicating the next block to read from each run. The “+∞” markers denote end-of-run conditions, ensuring the merge terminates correctly when all data is processed.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page404_img1.p.caption: This figure introduces the run generation algorithm, a key technique in external sorting that constructs longer initial runs than conventional methods. It leverages a loser tree data structure with k nodes (where k is the number of input streams) to enable parallel input, output, and internal processing, which is typically infeasible with standard internal sorting. The algorithm assumes sufficient internal memory to build the loser tree and uses two input and two output buffers to overlap I/O operations, improving efficiency.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page405_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates a loser tree data structure used for efficient tournament-based sorting, where each node stores a record’s key and run number. The tree’s k nodes represent tournament matches, with `tree[i].loser` tracking the losing record and `tree[i].data.run` indicating the run to which the record belongs. The algorithm maintains `current_run` and `winner_run` to manage output sequencing, while `max_runs` and `last_key` track the total runs generated and the key of the last output record, respectively. The `run_generation` function processes input records by replaying the tournament, outputting winners, and updating the tree state to generate sorted runs.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page406_img1.p.caption: This code snippet implements a tournament tree algorithm for external sorting, where nodes represent runs (sorted subsequences) and track their keys and run identifiers. The core components include a winner/loser tree structure, where the winner node holds the smallest key among its children, and the loser node is updated based on run comparisons. The algorithm progresses by advancing the winner node to the next run and adjusting the tree structure by promoting the loser node if its run is smaller or equal to the winner’s run and its key is smaller, maintaining the heap property for efficient selection of the next smallest key. The key relationship is that the tree dynamically reorganizes to ensure the smallest available key is always at the root, enabling optimal external merge sort performance.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page407_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates a loser tree data structure used in external sorting to efficiently merge multiple sorted runs. The key components include a tournament tree where each node stores a winner and loser record, with the loser tree initialized to handle fictitious run 0. The algorithm progresses by repeatedly comparing records, outputting the winner (lowest key), and updating the tree structure to maintain the correct tournament state for the next comparison. The `last_key` variable ensures non-decreasing output order, and the algorithm terminates when the last run is exhausted.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page408_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates optimal merge patterns in external sorting, where merge trees represent strategies for combining sorted runs of varying sizes. Key components include internal nodes (round) representing merge operations and external nodes (square) representing initial runs. The weighted external path length, calculated as the sum of (run size × distance from root), determines total merge time, with optimal trees minimizing this cost. The example demonstrates that merging smaller runs first reduces the total number of record comparisons.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page408_img2.p.caption: This figure depicts a binary tree, a non-linear data structure where each node has at most two children (left and right). The root node is at the top, with nodes 2 and 4 as its left and right children, respectively, and node 15 as the right child of the root’s left child. The tree exhibits hierarchical relationships, with parent-child links defining the structure, and node 5 is the right child of the root’s left child. This structure supports operations like traversal, search, and insertion, foundational to algorithms in computer science.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page408_img3.p.caption: This figure depicts a binary tree, a hierarchical data structure where each node has at most two children (left and right). The root node is at the top, with two subtrees: the left subtree contains nodes 2 and 4, and the right subtree contains nodes 5 and 15. The tree demonstrates parent-child relationships, where each non-leaf node directly connects to its descendants, forming a rooted, acyclic structure.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page409_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates Huffman coding’s greedy algorithm for constructing an optimal prefix code tree by minimizing weighted external path length. The key components are a min-heap initialized with singleton nodes (each weighted by input frequencies) and two operations: `least` (extract-min) to select the two lowest-weight nodes, and `insert` (insert) to merge them into a new internal node. The algorithm repeatedly merges the two smallest subtrees until one tree remains, ensuring the final tree’s weighted path length is minimal, directly optimizing data compression efficiency.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page40_img1.pn.caption: This figure illustrates the stack frame overhead for a single recursive function call, detailing the space consumed by parameters (float `list[]` and integer `n`) and the return address. The total space per call is 6 bytes, reflecting the memory allocated for activation records during recursion. This breakdown is foundational for analyzing space complexity, as it quantifies the memory footprint that scales with recursion depth. The figure directly supports understanding how recursive algorithms consume stack space, a critical factor in performance analysis.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page410_img1.p.caption: This code implements the Huffman algorithm using a min-heap to construct an optimal prefix code tree. The key components are a min-heap storing single-node trees, the `least()` function to extract the two trees with minimum weights, and the `insert()` function to reinsert the newly formed combined tree. The algorithm repeatedly combines the two smallest trees, maintaining the heap invariant, until one tree remains, resulting in an optimal binary tree for data compression. The asymptotic time complexity is O(n log n), dominated by heap operations.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page411_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates the step-by-step construction of a Huffman tree, a fundamental data structure for optimal prefix coding. The tree is built by repeatedly merging the two nodes with the smallest frequencies (or weights), forming a binary tree where internal nodes represent merged sets and leaf nodes represent original symbols. The key relationship is that the tree’s structure minimizes the weighted path length, directly optimizing for average code length in entropy encoding. The depicted merges (a)–(e) show how initial symbols (e.g., 5, 2, 3) are progressively combined into larger nodes (e.g., 39) to form the final optimal encoding tree.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page411_img4.p.caption: This figure depicts a binary search tree (BST) with nodes containing integer values. The root node is 23, with left child 10 and right child 13; node 10 has left child 5 and right child 5. Key BST properties are violated: the right child of 10 (5) is less than 10, and the right child of the root (13) is less than 23 — violating the BST invariant that all nodes in the left subtree are less than the root and all nodes in the right subtree are greater than the root. The tree is not a valid BST due to these violations.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page412_img1.p.caption: This section introduces foundational sorting algorithms and references seminal works on the topic. Key concepts include count sort, which leverages key-frequency counting for O(n) linear time complexity, and exchange sort (bubble sort), which operates via adjacent element swaps with O(n²) worst-case time. The text emphasizes algorithmic design trade-offs, such as the optimization of exchange sort using a flag to detect early termination when the list becomes sorted.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page413_img1.p.caption: **Shaker Sort** is a bidirectional bubble sort variant that alternates between bubbling the maximum element to the end and the minimum element to the beginning in each pass. Its major components include two nested loops for forward and backward passes, with early termination conditions to optimize performance. The algorithm’s time complexity is O(n²) in the worst case, but it can be faster than standard bubble sort due to its ability to move elements toward their final positions more efficiently. The key relationship is that each pass reduces the unsorted region by one element on each end, similar to cocktail shaker mixing.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page414_img1.p.caption: This figure introduces the symbol table, an Abstract Data Type (ADT) used to manage name-attribute pairs in applications like compilers and databases. Its core operations—membership testing, attribute retrieval, modification, and insertion—require efficient key-based access. The symbol table’s structure and associated hash functions are critical for enabling fast lookups and updates, forming the foundation for hashing techniques discussed in the chapter.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page415_img1.p.caption: This figure defines a SymbolTable Abstract Data Type (ADT) with core operations: Create, IsIn, Find, Insert, and Delete, managing unique name-attribute pairs. The ADT’s efficiency hinges on choosing an underlying data structure; while binary search trees offer O(log n) operations, hashing provides average-case O(1) performance by mapping keys to array indices via a hash function. The text introduces hashing as the primary technique for efficient symbol table implementation, contrasting it with tree-based methods and dividing its study into static and dynamic hashing.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page416_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates static hashing, a technique that maps identifiers to fixed-size hash table buckets using a hash function. The key components are the hash table (partitioned into `b` buckets, each with `s` slots), the hash function `f(x)` that computes the home address, and the loading factor `α = n/(b·s)` that quantifies table utilization. Important relationships include synonymy (identifiers hashing to the same bucket) and overflow (when a bucket becomes full), which occur simultaneously when `s = 1`.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page417_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates a hash table with 26 buckets (slots 0–25) and two slots per bucket, demonstrating the concept of collision handling via chaining. The key components are the hash table structure, bucket slots, and identifiers (e.g., `acos`, `atan`, `float`, `ceil`) that map to specific buckets via a hash function. The critical relationship shown is that multiple identifiers can hash to the same bucket, causing collisions, which necessitates overflow management strategies like chaining or probing. The figure highlights the trade-off between simplicity (e.g., using the first character as a hash) and collision avoidance, which is central to efficient hash table design.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page418_img1.p.caption: This figure introduces two fundamental static hashing techniques: the mid-square method and division hashing. The mid-square hash function computes a bucket address by squaring the identifier and extracting middle bits, while division hashing uses the modulo operator with a table size M. Both methods aim to distribute keys uniformly, though division hashing requires careful selection of M (ideally a prime) to avoid bias, whereas mid-square benefits from using a table size that is a power of two.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page419_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates two collision-resolution techniques in hashing: shift folding and folding at the boundaries. Both methods partition an identifier into parts and combine them to compute a hash address, but they differ in how the parts are aligned and processed. Shift folding aligns the least significant bit of each part with the last part before summing, while folding at the boundaries reverses every other partition before addition. These methods aim to distribute hash values more uniformly and reduce collisions caused by character permutations.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page41_img1.pn.caption: This figure introduces **program step analysis** as a machine-independent method for estimating algorithmic complexity by counting syntactically or semantically meaningful execution units. The key components are the **program step** (a unit of computation whose execution time is independent of instance size) and the **step count** (the total number of such steps executed). The relationship between step count and input size (n) is derived by analyzing control flow: iterative summing requires 2n+3 steps, while recursive summing requires 2n+2 steps, demonstrating how algorithm structure directly impacts asymptotic complexity.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page420_img1.p.caption: This figure introduces linear open addressing, a collision resolution strategy in static hashing. It uses a one-dimensional array of struct elements, each containing a key field (e.g., a string) and other metadata, with the array size defined as a prime number (TABLE_SIZE = 13) to minimize clustering. The hash table is initialized to all empty slots (represented by an empty string), enabling overflow detection during insertion by checking for occupied slots. The key relationship is that the hash function maps keys to indices in the array, and linear probing resolves collisions by sequentially probing the next slot until an empty one is found.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page421_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates core hash table operations: initialization, key transformation, and hashing. The `init_table` function initializes buckets to `NULL`, while `transform` converts a string key into a natural number via additive character summation. The `hash` function applies the division method (modulus) to map the transformed key to a bucket index within the table. These components enable efficient key-to-slot mapping, though the simplistic additive transform may lead to collisions, necessitating collision resolution strategies like linear probing.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page422_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates linear probing, a collision resolution strategy in static hashing. The additive transformation computes hash values for identifiers, which are then mapped to buckets in a 13-slot table. When a collision occurs (e.g., 'function' hashes to the same bucket as 'if'), linear probing resolves it by sequentially checking the next bucket (hr[(f(x) + j) % TABLE_SIZE]) until an empty slot is found. The key relationship is between the hash function output, table size, and the probing sequence that determines placement during insertion.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page423_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates linear probing for collision resolution in hash tables. The key concept is that when a collision occurs, the algorithm sequentially probes the next bucket (modulo TABLE_SIZE) until an empty slot is found or the home bucket is revisited, indicating the table is full. The major components are the hash function, the probing sequence, and the table’s home bucket index. The critical relationship is that linear probing’s sequential search can lead to clustering, where adjacent slots become filled, degrading search performance as the table fills.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page424_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates linear probing in static hashing, where collisions are resolved by sequentially searching subsequent buckets (e.g., bucket 0 → 1 → 2 → ...). The hash table has 26 buckets, each with one slot, and identifiers are inserted based on the first character of their name, leading to clustering as shown (e.g., 'atol' requires 9 probes). The table demonstrates that linear probing’s average search cost is low (1.36 probes for α=0.42) but can degrade to worst-case performance due to cluster formation. This motivates the use of quadratic probing, which employs a quadratic function to space out probes and reduce clustering.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page425_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates the generation of prime numbers in the form 4j + 3, which are commonly used as table sizes in hash tables to minimize clustering and improve distribution. The table lists primes (e.g., 3, 7, 11) alongside their corresponding j values (0, 1, 2), demonstrating how these primes are systematically derived. This concept is foundational for selecting optimal hash table sizes in open addressing schemes like linear probing, where prime sizes help reduce collisions and improve load factor efficiency. The primes shown are also relevant to the context of rehashing and random probing strategies discussed in the surrounding text.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page426_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates static hashing with chaining, a collision resolution technique where overflow elements are stored in linked lists at each bucket. Key components include the `element` struct (storing keys and other data), the `list-pointer` type (for chaining), and the `hash-table` array of pointers to these lists. The performance is governed by the load factor α (n/b), with expected probes of ~1 + α/2, and chaining outperforms linear probing for general use cases.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page427_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates chaining, a collision resolution technique in hash tables where collided keys are stored in linked lists at the same bucket. The key components are the hash table `ht[]`, each bucket containing a pointer to a linked list (`list-pointer`), and the `chain-insert` function that manages insertion via a `trail` pointer to maintain list integrity. The relationship is that the hash function maps keys to indices, and when collisions occur, the `chain-insert` function traverses the chain to check for duplicates and appends new nodes, maintaining O(1) average insertion time with proper load factor management.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page428_img1.p.caption: This figure presents a comparative analysis of average bucket accesses for various hash functions under different load factors (α = n/b) and overflow handling methods (chaining vs. open addressing). The key components are the hash functions (mid-square, division, shift fold, bound fold, digit analysis) and the performance metrics (average comparisons per retrieval) under varying load densities. The data reveals that chaining generally outperforms open addressing at lower loads, while open addressing becomes more efficient at higher loads for certain hash functions, highlighting the trade-off between collision handling strategies and load factor. The theoretical framework in the accompanying text formalizes these empirical results using probabilistic analysis of expected comparisons.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page429_img1.p.caption: This figure presents asymptotic approximations for expected search and insertion costs (Uₙ, Sₙ) in hash tables under three collision resolution strategies: rehashing, random probing, and quadratic probing (all yielding similar performance), and chaining. The key components are the load factor α (n/b, where n is the number of entries and b is the table size) and the expected number of comparisons per chain (Uₙ). For chaining, the analysis shows that the expected search cost Sₙ is approximately 1 + α/2, while for the other methods, Sₙ scales logarithmically with α, indicating that chaining becomes less efficient as the load factor increases. The derivation for chaining assumes uniform distribution of identifiers across chains and sequential insertion at the end of chains.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page42_img1.pn.caption: This figure illustrates step counting as a performance analysis technique to quantify algorithmic complexity. The key components include explicit `count++` statements inserted to tally operations, demonstrating how control flow (loop iterations, assignments, returns) contributes to total step count. The text highlights a critical relationship: while recursive functions may have fewer steps, they often incur higher per-step overhead, leading to worse overall runtime performance than iterative counterparts.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page430_img1.p.caption: This figure presents exercises on collision resolution strategies in static hashing, specifically linear probing and quadratic probing. Key components include hash functions, bucket addressing, and probing sequences that define how elements are placed and retrieved when collisions occur. The exercises highlight critical relationships: linear probing’s inefficiency with deletions due to tombstone ambiguity, and quadratic probing’s deterministic probing sequence that ensures uniform coverage of the table under specific conditions.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page431_img1.p.caption: This figure presents advanced hashing concepts, including a hash function using XOR and middle bits, symbol table design for O(1) operations via auxiliary arrays, set equivalence via subset testing, and linear open addressing analysis. Key components are the XOR-based hash function, two-array symbol table structure, and the load factor α in open addressing. Important relationships involve the hash function’s uniformity under random identifiers, the O(n + m) time complexity for set equivalence, and the approximation of average search cost Sn using α.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page432_img1.p.caption: This figure introduces static hashing as a foundational data structure for database management systems (DBMS), emphasizing its role in enabling fast access to large datasets. The key components include a hash table with fixed-size buckets, where identifiers are mapped via a hash function, and chaining is used to handle collisions. The operations—INIT, ENTERB, LEAVEB, RETRIEVE, and ISINB—illustrate core CRUD functionality for managing identifiers within a hierarchical block structure, with RETRIEVE and ISINB providing lookup capabilities based on identifier existence. The figure implicitly contrasts static hashing with dynamic hashing, highlighting the latter’s need for extensibility to accommodate variable data volumes without sacrificing retrieval speed.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page433_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates dynamic hashing using a trie (prefix tree) to manage variable-sized files with identifiers of fixed bit-length. The key concept is that identifiers are partitioned into pages based on their low-order bits, with branching decisions determined by subsequent bits in the identifier’s binary representation. The trie structure enables efficient, hierarchical lookup by following bit sequences from root to leaf, where each leaf node points to a page containing matching identifiers. This approach minimizes page accesses by dynamically organizing data based on identifier prefixes.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page434_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates dynamic hashing using a trie data structure to manage variable-length identifiers with overflow handling. The trie’s internal nodes represent branching decisions based on identifier prefixes, while leaf nodes store page pointers for actual data. Insertion of new identifiers (e.g., c5, c1) triggers overflow, necessitating node splitting and structural rebalancing to maintain efficient access and storage. The key relationship is that overflow management dynamically adjusts the trie’s height and node distribution to accommodate new entries without requiring pre-allocated space.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page434_img2.p.caption: This diagram illustrates a directed acyclic graph (DAG), a fundamental data structure in computer science used to model dependencies and precedence relationships. Key components include nodes (vertices) representing entities and directed edges (arcs) indicating ordered relationships or transitions. The acyclic nature ensures no cycles exist, enabling efficient topological sorting and critical applications in scheduling, data processing pipelines, and dependency resolution. The arrows denote directed flow, with edges pointing from a prerequisite to its dependent node.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page435_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates extendible hashing, a dynamic indexing technique that addresses overflow and skewed distribution in trie-based data structures. Key components include a directory of page pointers indexed by the low-order bits of identifiers and a hash function that maps keys to directory entries via uniform hashing to avoid clustering. The directory grows dynamically as pages are added, enabling efficient page retrieval in two steps: hash lookup followed by pointer resolution.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page436_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates dynamic hashing via trie collapsing into directory structures, where the number of bits used for key partitioning (2, 3, or 4 bits) determines the directory depth and bucket allocation. The key components are the hierarchical directory nodes (e.g., 00, 01, 10, 11) that map to buckets (a0, b0, c2, etc.), with each level representing a bit prefix of the key. The relationship is that as the key space grows, the directory expands dynamically by adding levels, and overflow handling involves rehashing identifiers using a family of hash functions that vary the number of bits used for partitioning.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page437_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates dynamic hashing using a directory-based approach with buddy pages for overflow management. Key components include a directory of pointers to pages, each page holding a variable number of identifiers (up to p), and a global depth tracking the trie’s height. The system dynamically adjusts directory size via splitting (when overflow occurs) or compression (when underutilized), maintaining O(log n) search complexity through a compressed trie structure.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page438_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates dynamic hashing, a technique for managing variable-sized data structures by dynamically allocating and deallocating memory pages. Key components include a hash function that maps keys to page addresses, a buddy system for page allocation (via `buddy` and `coalesce`), and operations for search (`pgsearch`), insertion (`enter`), and deletion (`pgdelete`). The `buddy` relationship enables efficient memory coalescing by complementing the leading bit of a page’s address, while `size` determines page capacity, supporting scalable storage management.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page439_img1.p.caption: This code snippet implements core operations for a hash-based file organization system, specifically focusing on insertion, deletion, and lookup within a hierarchical directory structure. The `find` function uses a hash function and directory lookup to locate a record's page, while `insert` handles overflow by splitting pages and updating the global depth to manage directory growth. The `delete` function is partially shown, indicating a need to locate and remove records, maintaining structural integrity. Key relationships involve the hash function mapping keys to directory indices, the directory managing page pointers, and the page structure handling record storage and overflow via splitting.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page43_img1.pn.caption: This figure illustrates the concept of **program analysis via step counting** to quantify computational complexity. The key components are the **step count table**, which records the **frequency** (execution count) of each statement, and the **total steps** derived by multiplying frequency by steps/execution (s/e). The relationship is that summing total steps across all statements yields the function’s overall computational cost, enabling comparison of algorithmic efficiency.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page440_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates dynamic hashing, specifically directory-based dynamic hashing, which guarantees retrieval with only two disk accesses by using a directory of pointers to variable-sized buckets. The key components include the directory (storing bucket pointers), buckets (containing actual records), and a coalescing mechanism (pgdelete and coalesce) to reclaim space when buckets are deleted. The analysis derives space utilization and leaf node count (L(k)) using combinatorial probability, showing that optimal space usage requires handling overflow by splitting buckets and redistributing keys probabilistically.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page441_img1.p.caption: This text discusses hash table overflow management and space utilization optimization. Key components include the directory (storing pointers), main pages (holding records), and overflow pages (handling overflow records). The analysis shows that overflow pages increase storage utilization (up to 75% with redistribution) but degrade retrieval time, necessitating strategies like monitoring space utilization or directory splitting to maintain efficiency.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page442_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates the trade-off between directory size and storage efficiency in extendible hashing, showing that directory overhead grows superlinearly with record count *n* and inversely with page size *p*. The key components are the directory (mapping bucket addresses) and variable-sized subpages (containing identifiers), with the relationship that directory growth is logarithmic in *n* but linear in the number of distinct bucket addresses. The figure highlights that for large *n* and small *p*, directory size becomes prohibitive, necessitating auxiliary storage. This motivates dynamic directory management strategies to maintain performance.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page443_img1.p.caption: Directoryless dynamic hashing, also known as linear hashing, eliminates the need for a separate directory by using a trie to map hash values directly to contiguous memory pages. Each hash function output is a unique page address, ensuring one-to-one mapping without indirection. The trie structure organizes pages hierarchically, with internal nodes representing bit prefixes and leaf nodes pointing to actual data pages. This design enables efficient insertion and deletion by splitting pages and extending the trie, maintaining a balanced structure without requiring directory updates.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page443_img2.p.caption: This diagram illustrates a binary decision tree, a fundamental data structure used in algorithms for decision-making and search operations. Each internal node represents a binary choice (0 or 1), with edges directing to child nodes, forming a hierarchical structure that terminates in leaf nodes (outputs). The tree’s structure enables efficient traversal and decision paths, commonly applied in algorithms like Huffman coding, binary search, and decision trees in machine learning.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page444_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates directoryless dynamic hashing, where page addresses are determined by hash function outputs. Key components include the hash table (initially 4 pages addressed by 2 bits), overflow pages for handling collisions, and the expansion phase triggered when a page becomes full. The critical relationship is that hash values determine page placement, and overflow chains are formed when a page cannot accommodate new entries, necessitating page splits and address bit expansion to maintain load balance.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page445_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates the directoryless hashing expansion phase, where pages are partitioned into three regions: those already split, those awaiting split, and those newly added. The key concept is dynamic rehashing: when a new page is added, identifiers are redistributed across the current and new pages using a modified hash function, which requires r+1 bits for pages beyond the q-th position. The relationship between page addresses and split phases is critical—pages left of q use r bits, while those right of r use r+1 bits, and the hash function’s range ensures overflow handling via rehashing.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page446_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates a modified hash function for dynamic hashing, specifically addressing overflow handling during directory expansion. The key concept is adaptive bucket assignment: the function uses a threshold `q` to determine whether to use the current hash value or an incremented version, directing keys to a different bucket to mitigate skew. The critical relationship is between the hash value, the directory’s current state (via `r`), and the overflow pointer mechanism, which ensures keys are routed to appropriate pages even as the directory grows. This approach helps balance load by delaying overflow chains until later splits, improving space utilization.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page447_img1.p.caption: This figure introduces dynamic hashing, a technique for managing variable-sized hash tables by resizing the directory structure on demand. Key components include a directory (often implemented as a linked tree) that maps hash values to bucket pointers, and a hash function that must be adaptable to accommodate directory expansion. The critical relationship is that directory growth is triggered by overflow, and the hash function must be rewritten to maintain consistent bucket mapping as the directory’s size and structure evolve.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page448_img1.p.caption: This text discusses dynamic hashing, a data structure technique for managing variable-sized data in databases. Key components include hash functions, overflow handling, and page splitting mechanisms that adapt to changing data volumes. The core concept involves deferring page splits and managing overflows internally to optimize storage utilization, though this introduces a tradeoff with access time. Important relationships exist between storage efficiency, access latency, and the dynamic reorganization of hash buckets to maintain performance under load.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page449_img1.p.caption: This figure introduces min-max heaps, a specialized complete binary tree data structure designed as a double-ended priority queue. Its key concept is alternating min and max levels, where min nodes on min levels hold the minimum key in their subtree and max nodes on max levels hold the maximum key in their subtree. The root resides on a min level, enabling efficient extraction of both minimum and maximum elements, which is the core relationship enabling its dual-priority functionality.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page44_img1.pn.caption: This figure illustrates algorithmic performance analysis through step counting, a foundational technique in computational complexity. Program 1.15 demonstrates nested iteration over a 2D array for matrix addition, while Program 1.16 instrumented with `count++` statements quantifies the execution cost by tracking individual operations. The key relationship is that the total step count—rows × cols—directly reflects the algorithm’s time complexity, O(n²), enabling precise performance evaluation and comparison.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page450_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates a min-max heap, a complete binary tree data structure where each level alternates between min-heap and max-heap properties. Key components include nodes labeled with keys (e.g., 7, 40, 10) and explicit level markings (“min” or “max”) indicating the heap property enforced at that depth. The critical relationship is that for any node, its value must be greater than or equal to all values in its min-level descendants and less than or equal to all values in its max-level descendants, enabling efficient insertion by verifying only the path from the new node to the root.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page451_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates a min-max heap, a complete binary tree where nodes alternate between min and max levels, enforcing that each max node’s key is at least as large as all nodes in its path to the root and all nodes in its path to the leaves. The key components include the heap structure, level functions to determine node type, and insertion procedures that use `verify_max` and `verify_min` to maintain heap properties. The insertion of a new element (key 40) triggers a structural rearrangement, moving the displaced element to a new position while preserving the min-max heap invariant through level-based verification.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page452_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates min-max heap insertion, a balanced tree structure enforcing alternating min and max heap properties at each level. Key components include the root (min), alternating min/max subtrees, and insertion paths that maintain the min-max heap invariant. The insertion process involves placing the new element and then verifying/repairing the heap by moving nodes along alternating levels to preserve the property. The O(log n) complexity arises from the depth of the tree, which limits the number of level adjustments needed.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page453_img1.p.caption: This code implements min-max heap insertion, a balanced binary tree structure where each level alternates between min-heap and max-heap properties. The algorithm determines the insertion level (min or max) based on the parent node’s level, then performs a corresponding heapify operation (verify-min or verify-max) to restore the heap invariant. The key relationship is that the heap’s level-based ordering enforces a strict hierarchy: min-level nodes must be smaller than their children, while max-level nodes must be larger, creating a self-balancing structure that supports efficient insertion and deletion.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page454_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates the structural state of a min-max heap after a delete-min operation, showing the resulting tree with alternating min and max levels. The key components include the root (12, a max node), internal min nodes (70, 40), and leaf nodes (45, 50, 9, 30, 20, 10, 15), maintaining the heap property where min nodes are children of max nodes and vice versa. The accompanying `verify_max` function demonstrates the reinsertion process by bubbling the new element upward from its insertion point, comparing it with grandparent nodes to restore the heap invariant. The relationship between the heap’s hierarchical structure and the verification algorithm highlights the dynamic maintenance required for min-max heap operations.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page455_img1.p.caption: This figure details the `delete-min` operation in a min-max heap, a complete binary tree where each node is a max-heap of its children. The algorithm identifies the minimum key by traversing the root’s children and grandchildren, then either inserts the new element into the identified node or swaps it with the node’s key if the node is a max-heap. The operation maintains heap properties by recursively reinserting the element into the appropriate sub-heap, ensuring O(log n) time complexity through the tree’s height.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page456_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates a min-max heap after deleting the root (key 9), demonstrating its structural reorganization. The heap maintains alternating min and max levels, where min levels contain the smallest keys and max levels contain the largest keys among their subtrees. The deletion triggers a cascade of adjustments to restore heap properties, with the new root (key 40) now occupying a max level, and the heap’s structure is reconfigured to preserve the min-max ordering invariant. The figure highlights the dynamic nature of min-max heaps during deletion operations.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page457_img1.p.caption: This function implements the `delete_min` operation on a min-max heap, a specialized heap structure that maintains two heap-ordering properties simultaneously. It removes the root (minimum element), replaces it with the last element, and then restores the min-max heap property by traversing down the tree, comparing with children and grandchildren, and swapping as needed to maintain the correct ordering. The algorithm leverages the heap’s array-based representation and the min-max heap’s specific ordering rules (cases 2b, 2c) to ensure the heap invariant is preserved after deletion.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page458_img1.p.caption: A deap is a complete binary tree implementing a double-ended priority queue by combining a min-heap (left subtree) and a max-heap (right subtree). Its key invariant, property (4), ensures that for every node in the min-heap, its corresponding node in the max-heap (or its parent) has a key that is greater than or equal to the min-heap node’s key, enabling efficient extraction of both minimum and maximum elements. This structure maintains logarithmic time complexity for insert, delete-min, and delete-max operations while simplifying the underlying algorithms compared to min-max heaps.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page458_img2.p.caption: This figure depicts a binary tree, a hierarchical data structure where each node has at most two children (left and right). The root node is unlabelled, with subtrees rooted at nodes 5 and 45, forming a binary search tree (BST) where values in the left subtree are less than the root and values in the right subtree are greater. Key relationships include parent-child links (e.g., 5 is parent of 10 and 8) and sibling relationships (e.g., 10 and 8 are siblings under root 5), with leaf nodes (15, 19, 9, 30, 20, 40) having no children.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page459_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates the **deap** data structure, a hybrid of min- and max-heaps that enables efficient double-ended priority queue operations. Its key components include an implicit binary tree representation stored in an array, where the min element resides at the root of the min-heap and the max element at the root of the max-heap, with both heaps sharing the same underlying tree structure. The critical relationship is that the position of a node in the min-heap maps to a corresponding position in the max-heap via the formula j = i + 2^⌊log₂i⌋−1, ensuring both heap properties are maintained simultaneously.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page45_img1.pn.caption: This figure illustrates algorithmic analysis through step counting, a foundational technique in computational complexity. The step count table quantifies the execution frequency and total computational cost of each statement in the `sum` function, revealing a linear time complexity of O(n) due to the single loop iterating n times. The table’s structure maps statement execution to step counts, enabling precise cost analysis and simplification of the function’s asymptotic behavior.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page460_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates a min-heap (deap) data structure, a complete binary tree where each parent node is less than or equal to its children, ensuring the minimum element is at the root. The key components include the root (value 5), internal nodes (10, 8, 45, 25), and leaf nodes (15, 19, 9, 30, 20, and an empty slot marked 'j'), with indices 'i' and 'j' indicating positions for heap operations. The heap property is maintained through comparisons and swaps during insertion, as shown in Figure 9.8 after inserting the value 4, which triggers a percolate-up to restore the heap invariant.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page460_img2.p.caption: This figure depicts a binary search tree (BST), a hierarchical data structure where each node has at most two children and satisfies the BST property: all values in the left subtree are less than the node’s value, and all values in the right subtree are greater. The root node is 4, with left subtree rooted at 5 and right subtree rooted at 45; each subtree recursively maintains the BST invariant. Key relationships include parent-child links (e.g., 4 → 5 and 4 → 45) and the ordering constraint that enables efficient search, insertion, and deletion operations in O(log n) average time.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page461_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates a max-heap data structure after inserting the value 30, demonstrating heap ordering where each parent node is greater than or equal to its children. The key components include the root (5), internal nodes (10, 8, 45, 30), and leaf nodes (15, 19, 9, 20, 25, 40), with the heap property maintained through structural relationships. The accompanying text defines supporting functions like `max_heap(n)` and `max_partner(n)` for heap position validation and parent-child mapping, and describes `min_insert`/`max_insert` operations that maintain heap structure by percolating elements toward the root. The figure serves as a visual reference for understanding heap operations and their implementation details.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page462_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates the `deap-insert` function, which implements insertion into a deap (a double-ended heap) by leveraging its dual-structure of a max-heap on the min side and a min-heap on the max side. The algorithm determines the insertion position `i` via `max-partner` or `min-partner` based on the heap’s current state, then performs a standard heap insertion (`max-insert` or `min-insert`) after potentially swapping the new element with the element at position `i` to maintain heap invariants. The key relationship is that the deap’s structure allows efficient insertion at either end by dynamically choosing the appropriate heap component and adjusting the insertion point using the partner function, which maps positions across the two heaps.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page463_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates the `delete_min` operation on a min-heap, a fundamental heap data structure. The algorithm removes the root (minimum element), replaces it with the last leaf, and restores the heap property by percolating down the smaller child. Key components include the heap array, indices for parent/child navigation, and a temporary variable for the removed root. The operation maintains the min-heap invariant by repeatedly swapping the current node with its smaller child until the heap property is restored.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page464_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates a deap (double-ended heap) after a delete-min operation, demonstrating its structure as a hybrid of min- and max-heaps. The key components include the root node (8), which maintains min-heap property with its left subtree, and the right subtree (45, 25, 40), which maintains max-heap property. The important relationship is that the deap’s root is the minimum element, and the delete-min operation preserves the deap invariant by replacing the root with the last element and performing necessary adjustments to restore both heap properties. The operation’s time complexity is O(log n), matching the height of the deap.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page465_img1.p.caption: This figure introduces leftist trees as an extended binary tree data structure designed to efficiently support the combine operation for merging priority queues. Key components include internal nodes (original binary tree nodes) and external nodes (square nodes replacing empty subtrees), with the critical property that the shortest path from any internal node to an external node is minimized. The structure ensures that combine and standard priority queue operations run in logarithmic time by maintaining a heap property and leveraging the shortest path recurrence to guide tree balancing.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page466_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates leftist trees, a self-balancing binary tree variant optimized for merge operations. The key concept is the “shortest” path from a node to a leaf, defined recursively as 0 for external nodes and 1 + min(shortest(left), shortest(right)) for internal nodes. The defining property of a leftist tree is that for every internal node, the shortest path to the left child is at least as long as to the right child, ensuring the right spine is the shortest, which facilitates efficient merging.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page466_img2.p.caption: This figure depicts two disconnected binary trees, each with a root node (A and G) and hierarchical parent-child relationships. Node A has children B and C, while B has children D and E, and C has child F; similarly, G has children H and I, and H has child J. The structure illustrates tree traversal paths and subtree relationships, but the absence of a connecting edge indicates they form separate, non-interacting components.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page466_img3.p.caption: This figure depicts a binary tree structure, specifically illustrating a heap (likely a min-heap) where each node’s value is less than or equal to its children’s values. The key components include the root node A, internal nodes B and C, leaf nodes D, E, and F, and the square placeholder indicating a missing child. The numbers beside edges (2 and 1) represent edge weights or path costs, suggesting this may model a weighted tree or a shortest-path problem, with A as the source node. The parent-child relationships define the tree hierarchy, and the weights imply potential optimization paths from A to leaves.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page466_img4.p.caption: This figure depicts a binary tree structure, where node G serves as the root and has two children, H and I, each with a weight of 1. Node H has one child, J (also weighted 1), while node I has two leaf nodes (empty squares), indicating a complete binary tree with a total of 7 nodes. The weights on the edges suggest this may represent a Huffman coding tree, where the path lengths from the root encode character frequencies, and the leaf nodes correspond to encoded symbols.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page467_img1.p.caption: This figure defines and implements leftist trees, a type of heap structure optimized for efficient merging. Key components include nodes with `left-child`, `right-child`, `shortest` (path length to nearest leaf), and `data` fields, where external nodes are represented implicitly via `NULL` pointers. The `shortest` field enables O(log n) path-length balancing, ensuring the rightmost root-to-leaf path is minimal, which is critical for maintaining optimal merge performance. The structure supports min-leftist trees (a heap variant) where each node’s key is ≤ its children’s keys.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page468_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates min-leftist trees, a type of heap-structured binary tree optimized for efficient merge operations. Key components include nodes storing keys and their associated “shortest” path lengths (the number of edges to the nearest leaf), with the root holding the minimum key. The critical relationship is that the tree maintains a leftist property: the rightmost path is always the shortest, enabling O(log n) time complexity for insert and delete-min operations via a combine operation that merges two leftist trees by recursively combining their rightmost paths and then restoring the leftist property through left-right subtree swaps.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page469_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates the construction of a leftist tree through a min-union operation, which combines two leftist trees by recursively merging their rightmost paths. The key components are the min-union function, which ensures the root of each subtree has the smallest key, and the min-combine function, which enforces the leftist property (shortest(left-child) ≥ shortest(right-child)) by interchanging subtrees as needed. The operation maintains O(log n) time complexity by leveraging the logarithmic depth of the rightmost paths in leftist trees. The resulting structure is a valid leftist tree that preserves the heap property and the leftist invariant.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page46_img1.pn.caption: This figure illustrates step counting as a foundational technique for analyzing algorithmic time complexity. Figure 1.3 demonstrates recursive function analysis, where the total steps (2n+2) are derived from summing the frequencies of individual statements, revealing linear growth with input size n. Figure 1.4 shows matrix addition, where nested loops yield a quadratic step count (2rows·cols+2rows+cols), highlighting how nested iteration scales with input dimensions. Both examples show that step count tables map source code to mathematical expressions that quantify computational cost as a function of input size.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page470_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates the process of combining min-leftist trees, a self-adjusting heap data structure optimized for merge operations. Each node maintains a `shortest` field storing the length of the shortest path to a leaf in its subtree, enabling O(log n) merge complexity. The diagrams show how two leftist trees (a) and (b) are merged into a single tree (c), preserving the leftist property (the root’s left subtree is always the “heaviest” or longest), and then demonstrate a reorganization (d) to maintain this property after deletion. The key relationship is that the `shortest` field facilitates efficient path-length comparisons during merging and deletion, ensuring the heap property is preserved through structural adjustments.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page471_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates the `min-combine` operation for leftist trees, a key heap structure that merges two min-heaps by recursively combining their rightmost paths. The operation leverages the leftist tree’s structural invariant (the right path length is minimized) to ensure O(log n) time complexity. It uses a `min-union` helper function to merge the trees, then nullifies the input tree `*b` to maintain the invariant. The operation is foundational for supporting efficient heap operations like `delete-min` and `insert`, which rely on similar path-traversal and subtree-combining techniques.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page472_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates the `min-union` operation for leftist trees, a key component in binomial heap implementation. The algorithm recursively combines two min leftist trees by attaching the smaller root as the left child of the larger root, preserving the leftist property (the right subtree is always the shortest). It maintains the `shortest` field to track the shortest path from the root to a leaf, ensuring the heap property is preserved during union. The operation’s time complexity is O(log n), which is optimal for this data structure.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page473_img1.p.caption: This figure introduces binomial heaps, a data structure composed of a collection of min-trees (B-heaps), where each node stores its degree, child, left-link, right-link, and data fields. The left- and right-link fields maintain a doubly linked circular list of siblings, and the roots themselves form a doubly linked circular list, enabling O(1) insertion and delete-min operations with amortized time complexity. The structure leverages the properties of min-trees to support efficient heap operations while maintaining a logarithmic bound on the number of trees.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page474_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates a B-heap, a variant of a binomial heap where each min-tree is represented as a doubly linked circular list. The structure consists of a root pointer to the min-tree with the smallest key, and each node contains child and parent pointers (shown with broken and solid arrows, respectively). The key sets denote the elements within each circular list, and the pointer `a` identifies the current B-heap’s root. The design enables O(1) insertion and combination operations by leveraging the circular list structure and direct pointer comparisons.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page474_img2.p.caption: This figure depicts a binary tree, a hierarchical data structure where each node has at most two children (left and right). The root node is 1, with children 12 and 16; node 12 further branches to 15 and 30, and node 7 branches to 9. The tree exhibits parent-child relationships, with edges indicating directional connectivity from parent to child nodes, and node 20 is a leaf node (no children).

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page475_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates the structural transformation of a B-heap during min-element deletion. The key concept is maintaining heap properties by replacing the root (minimum element) with the last node from the doubly linked circular list of min-trees, then restructuring the remaining subtrees. Major components include the root node, its children (min-trees), and the sibling lists that maintain connectivity. The important relationship is that deletion preserves the B-heap invariant by reorganizing the forest of min-trees into a new valid B-heap structure.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page476_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates the min-tree joining operation in a binomial heap, a key data structure for efficient priority queue operations. The process merges two min-trees of equal degree by making the tree with the larger root a subtree of the other, thereby increasing the resulting tree’s degree by one and reducing the total number of min-trees. This operation is foundational to maintaining the heap’s structure during insertions and deletions, ensuring the heap property is preserved through controlled tree merging. The resulting structure, shown as a doubly linked circular list of roots, enables O(log n) time complexity for delete-min operations by facilitating efficient tree traversal and selection.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page477_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates the structure of a B-heap formed by merging two degree-two min-trees, demonstrating the consolidation phase of B-heap operations. The key components are the root node (12), its children (15 and 30), and the child of 15 (20), forming a valid min-heap where parent keys are less than or equal to child keys. The important relationship is that the B-heap maintains the min-heap property while allowing efficient deletion by restructuring the heap through min-tree joining and root list formation, as outlined in the accompanying deletion algorithm.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page477_img2.p.caption: This figure depicts a binary tree, a hierarchical data structure where each node has at most two children (left and right). The root node is 3, with subtrees rooted at 7 (left child) and 5 (middle child), and 4 (right child). Node 7 further branches to 8 and 9, with 8 having a child 10, illustrating parent-child relationships and tree traversal paths. The structure enables operations like search, insertion, and deletion with logarithmic complexity in balanced forms.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page478_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates the core mechanism for managing min-trees in binomial heaps during a scan, specifically handling the reorganization of trees of degree `p`. The key components are the `tree[degree]` array, which tracks the root of each degree’s binomial tree, and the `join-min-trees` operation, which merges trees of the same degree. The critical relationship is that after processing, the `tree[degree]` pointer is updated to point to the newly formed tree, while the original tree is marked as `NULL`, ensuring the heap maintains its structure for future operations. This process is foundational for achieving the amortized time complexity guarantees of binomial heaps.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page479_img1.p.caption: This figure analyzes the amortized cost of delete-min operations in a B-heap, demonstrating how a charging scheme assigns cost units to insert operations to cover the expense of future deletions. The key components are the `last-size` counter, the number of min-trees remaining after a deletion, and the `MAX_DEGREE` parameter, which bounds the time to initialize the tree structure. The analysis shows that the amortized cost of delete-min becomes O(log n) by charging each insert operation a number of units proportional to the number of min-trees it leaves behind, ensuring the total amortized cost of a sequence of operations remains O(i + c + d log n).

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page47_img1.pn.caption: This figure illustrates foundational algorithmic concepts in computational complexity analysis. It presents two functions: `print-matrix`, which demonstrates nested iteration over a 2D array, and `mult`, which implements matrix multiplication using triple nested loops. The accompanying text establishes that algorithmic complexity is defined by selecting relevant problem instance characteristics (e.g., matrix dimensions) and defining a "step" as a unit of computation (e.g., an addition or multiplication), enabling the derivation of time complexity functions.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page480_img1.p.caption: A Fibonacci heap is a data structure that supports insert, delete-min, and decrease-key operations in O(1) amortized time, and delete operations in O(log n) amortized time. It consists of a collection of min-heap-ordered trees, where each tree satisfies the min-heap property and each node maintains degree information. The key relationships involve linking trees via child pointers and maintaining a root list, enabling efficient consolidation during operations through lazy restructuring.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page481_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates the structural transformation of an F-heap after deleting a non-root node (12). The operation involves detaching the node’s children from its parent and merging them into the main root list, preserving the heap property. The resulting structure maintains the F-heap’s core components: a doubly linked list of min-tree roots and parent-child pointers, ensuring efficient reorganization without violating heap invariants. The deletion’s O(1) amortized cost stems from the absence of cascading adjustments, unlike delete-min operations.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page482_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates the structural transformation of a Fibonacci heap after a decrease-key operation. The key concept is that decreasing a node’s key may necessitate its removal from its parent’s subtree and insertion into the root list, potentially triggering cascading cuts. The major components shown are the min-tree roots (nodes 1, 6, 11, 20) and the subtree rooted at 1 (containing nodes 7, 12, 16, 30), with node 10 being cut and removed from its parent’s list. The relationship depicted is that the decrease-key operation maintains the heap property by reorganizing the structure, and the cascading cut mechanism (not shown here but described in the text) ensures the heap’s amortized efficiency by propagating cuts upward when necessary.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page483_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates the cascading cut mechanism in Fibonacci heaps during a decrease-key operation. The key concept is maintaining heap structure integrity by severing min-trees with `child-cut = FALSE` from their parent, propagating the cut upward until a root is found or a node with `child-cut = TRUE` is encountered. The major components are the min-tree (a rooted tree with a minimum key), the `child-cut` flag (indicating if a node is cut from its parent), and the doubly linked list of min-tree roots. The important relationship is that cascading cuts ensure the degree of any node remains bounded by O(log n), which underpins the O(log n) amortized cost of delete-min operations.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page484_img1.p.caption: This figure illustrates a cascading cut operation in a Fibonacci heap following a decrease-key operation. The key concept is maintaining heap properties through structural modifications: when a node’s key is decreased, if it violates the min-heap property relative to its parent, it is cut from its parent and potentially triggers a chain of cuts up the tree. The major components are the heap’s root list (containing nodes 2, 4, 6, 10, 14) and the tree structure (with nodes 4, 6, 8, 10, 12, 14, 16, 18, 30, 60), where the asterisk denotes a marked node. The important relationship is that cascading cuts ensure the heap’s potential function remains bounded, preserving amortized efficiency by transferring the cost of restructuring to future operations.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page485_img1.p.caption: This text analyzes amortized analysis of F-heaps, demonstrating that insert, combine, and decrease-key operations have O(1) amortized cost, while delete-min is O(log n). The key mechanism is modifying the `last-size` counter to track net changes in min-trees, enabling cost transfer from delete-min and decrease-key operations to insert operations. This adjustment ensures the amortized cost of delete-min becomes O(log n) by charging cascading cuts to delete and decrease-key operations, while insert remains O(1) as its actual and amortized costs are equal.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page486_img1.p.caption: This text describes the use of Fibonacci heaps (F-heaps) to optimize the single-source shortest path algorithm, leveraging their amortized efficiency for decrease-key and delete-min operations. The key components are the priority queue S (implemented as an F-heap) storing vertices with distance values, and the algorithm’s reliance on O(n log n + e) time complexity, which improves upon the O(n² log n + ne) time of the previous dynamic programming approach for sparse graphs. The relationship hinges on the F-heap’s ability to support these operations efficiently, enabling the algorithm to process each edge and vertex optimally.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page487_img1.p.caption: This figure presents exercises on F-heaps, a specialized heap data structure designed for efficient amortized operations. Key components include the min-tree structure, cascading cuts (which maintain heap properties by cutting excess children), and the modified cascading cut rule that triggers only after a fixed number k of children are cut. The exercises explore the theoretical underpinnings—recurrence relations for minimum node counts, amortized complexity analysis under modified rules, and practical implementation—highlighting the trade-offs between structural integrity and performance as k increases.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page488_img1.p.caption: This section summarizes key priority queue data structures and their applications in graph algorithms. It highlights min-max heaps, deap (double-ended heap), leftist trees, B-heaps, and F-heaps, each with distinct structural properties and performance characteristics. The text emphasizes their use in optimizing minimum cost spanning tree algorithms, noting asymptotic bounds involving β(e,n), log* n, and log log n. These structures enable efficient implementations of algorithms like Prim’s and Kruskal’s, particularly for large-scale network optimization problems.

img::images/books/DSA - 3_page489_img1.p.caption: This section introduces optimal binary search trees (BSTs) for static datasets, where the goal is to minimize the expected search cost.
